paragraphs.en: Hello everyone and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Our narrative has now reached the 4th millennium BC. This is an important moment in the story, Because this is the time in human history when people made two major inventions. First they invented Bronze, which is a type of metal much harder than copper. And this meant that humans could make metal objects that could be used for chopping and cutting, like axes and knives.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好！欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列课程。 目前我们已经讲到了公元前四千 年。 这是一个重要的时间节点， 因为在人类历史的这一时期，人类 有两项重大发明。 第一项发明就是青铜，是一种比铜 更坚硬的金属。 这意味着人类可以制作用于劈砍 和切割的金属工具，比如斧子和 刀。

paragraphs.en: And this allowed people to completely replace all of their stone tools with metal tools. We have left the stone age behind and entered the BronzeAge. Here is a timeline that I created showing us where we are in human history. This timeline includes the last 12,000 years. It starts at 10,000 BC, which is about the time the Natufians first began farming. The period from 10,000 to 5,000 BC is called the Neolithic, and the period from 5,000 to 3,000 BC is called the CopperAge. These seven thousand years were the topic of our last 10 lectures.
paragraphs.zh: 并且人类终于得以用金属工具完 全取代石制工具。 我们已经脱离石器时代，进入了青 铜时代。 我做了一条时间线，来显示此时在 人类历史上所处的位置。 这条时间线包含过去的12,000年。 它发端于公元前 10,000 年，这大 约是纳图夫人开始耕作的时期。 公元前 10,000 至 5,000 年叫做新 石器时代，而公元前 5,000 至公元 前 3,000 年被称为铜器时代。 这七千年就是我们过去 10 讲的主 题。

paragraphs.en: But today we are moving into a new period of history called the Bronze Age. This period lasted about 1800 years from 3000 to 1200 BC. The BronzeAge will be the focus of the next 20 lessons. So let's zoom in a little and focus on that time. This is a second timeline that I have created. Here, we are looking at the last 5500 years of history instead of the last 12,000. As with the last slide, the Bronze Age is the period highlighted in gold. The other huge invention, which marks the beginning of the Bronze Age, was the invention of writing.
paragraphs.zh: 但是今天我们进入了一个新的历 史阶段，叫做青铜时代。 这一阶段自公元前 3000 至公元前 1200 年，延续了约 1800 年。 接下来的 20 讲就将围绕青铜时代 展开。 那么就让我们将目光聚焦在那段 岁月吧。 这是我制作的第二条时间线。 在这张图上我们看到的不是过去 的 12,000 年，而是 5500 年。 同上一张图表一样，青铜时代用黄 色凸显了出来。 另外一项重大发明就是文字。文字 的出现标志着青铜时代的开始。

paragraphs.en: Writing meant that people could leave records, and names, and stories about their lives in their own words. Writing means that we no longer have to rely on archaeology alone, we can read what these ancient people had to say about life in their own words. Writing means the end of prehistory, and the beginning of true history. As far as we know, complex societies with writing, bronze tools, and the first true cities appeared around 3500 BC. These earliest civilizations always developed along river valleys, which made transportation and agriculture much easier.
paragraphs.zh: 有了文字，意味着人类可以用自己 的词语留名纪事。 出现文字还意味着我们无需仅仅 依赖考古来了解往事，我们可以直 接阅读古人用自己的话语书写的 生活。 文字为史前时代画上句点，为真正 的“历史”拉开序幕。 据我们所知，拥有文字、青铜工具， 和真正意义上的城邦的复杂社会 最早出现于公元前 3500 年。 这些最早的文明往往诞生于河谷 地带，因为这里有利于交通和农耕 的发展。

paragraphs.en: We will begin this series by looking at the two oldest civilizations in the world. Sumer, which developed along the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, and Egypt which developed along the Nile. We will look at the Sumerians first. The entire region around the Tigris and Euphrates rivers can also be called by another name:"Mesopotamia." Mesopotamia is a Greek word that means "The Land Between the Two Rivers." As I mentioned in our last series on the Neolithic, farming preceded complex societies and true cities by several thousand years.
paragraphs.zh: 在这个系列课程的一开始，我们先 来看看世界上两个最古老的文明。 它们就是发祥于底格里斯河和幼 发拉底河流域的苏美尔文明，以及 诞生于尼罗河流域的古埃及文明。 我们先来看看苏美尔人。 整个底格里斯河与幼发拉底河流 域还有一个别称：“美索不达米 亚”。美索不达米亚是一个希腊语 词汇，意思是“两河之间的土地”。 在上一个关于新石器时代的系列 课程中，我提到过，农耕的出现要 比复杂社会形态和真正城邦的出 现早了数千年。

paragraphs.en: And this was also true in Mesopotamia. So far, the oldest farming settlement that we have discovered in this part of the world is called Jarmo. Of course "Jarmo" is the name that archaeologists have given the site. The people who lived there did not yet have writing, so we don't know what they called themselves. Jarmo, was not located directly in the river valleys, it was located in the foothills of the Zagros mountains. And this makes sense because the river valleys themselves are mainly mud and water and don't have many stones or minerals for making tools. Here on the slide I have included a photograph of the Jarmo
paragraphs.zh: 这一点在美索不达米亚地区也不 例外。 目前为止，在该地区发现的最早的 农耕聚居区叫做“贾莫”。 当然，“贾莫”是考古学家对这个 遗址的命名。 这里的居民当时还没有文字，所以 他们如何称呼自己的家乡，我们也 不得而知。 贾莫没有直接建在河谷地带，而是 位于扎格罗斯山脚下。 这个选址是顺理成章的，因为河谷 地带主要有泥土和水，但是缺少用 于制作工具的岩石和矿物。 这张图片左侧展示了贾莫的考古 遗址，而右侧是这里出土的一些物

paragraphs.en: archaeological site on the left, and on the right is a collection of some of the items that have been discovered there. As you can see the people of Jarmo were quite skilled at making tools and items from the materials in their environment. It’s also interesting that some of the items from Jarmo were made from Shell or Obsidian. These materials cannot be found near Jarmo, and the nearest source was hundreds of kilometers away. So this is strong evidence that the people of Jarmo were engaged in long-distance trade. Jarmo thrived from about 7,000 to 5,000 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 品。 你可以看到，贾莫人非常善于利用 当地的材料制作工具和其他物品。 有趣的是，部分贾莫遗址出土的物 品是用贝壳或黑曜石制成的。 可是贾莫附近并没有这些材料，最 近的出产地也在数百千米之外。 因此这是一项有力的证据，证明贾 莫人在从事长途贸易。 贾莫繁盛于公元前 7,000-5,000 年。

paragraphs.en: During the next phase of Mesopotamian history, people began coming down out of the Zagros hills and directly settling the floodplains. The disadvantage to the floodplains was that rocks and minerals and wood were in short supply. But the big advantage was that the land was very, very fertile. And annual floods by the Tigris and Euphrates rivers continually replenished the soil. This period of transition from the foothills to the river valleys lasted from about 6,500 to 3,800 BC. Historians call this the Ubaid period. Large settlements like Ubaid, Hassuna, Samara and Halaf
paragraphs.zh: 在美索不达米亚历史的下一个阶 段，人们开始走出了扎格罗斯山， 直接定居到了河流的洪积平原上。 洪积平原的不利之处在于这里缺 乏岩石、矿物，和木材。 然而这里却有一个巨大的优势，那 就是土地非常、非常肥沃。 每年底格里斯河与幼发拉底河的 洪水持续为土壤带来养分。 这段从山脚向河谷迁居的历史阶 段从公元前6,500年持续到公元前 历史学家将这一时期称为欧贝德 时期。 一些大型聚居点，比如欧贝德、哈 苏纳、萨马拉，以及哈拉夫都是在

paragraphs.en: developed during this time. The key to this development was irrigation technology. By digging irrigation channels the Mesopotamians were able to water all of the land around the rivers. This irrigated river-valley land could then be farmed and this yielded huge crop surpluses. With all of this extra grain, people could afford to have many babies and large families. This in turn led to a population boom. First these settlements grew into villages with hundreds of people. Then these villages grew into towns with thousands of people. And then these towns grew into the
paragraphs.zh: 这一时期发展起来的。 而发展的关键就是灌溉技术。 通过挖掘灌溉水渠，美索不达米亚 人得以浇灌河流附近的所有土地。 这些灌溉过的河谷土地可以用来 耕作，继而生产出大量过剩的农作 物。 有了这些富余的粮食，人们就可以 养育更多孩子，养活更大的家庭。 这相应地带来了人口爆发式增长。 起初这些群落发展为有数百人的 村落。 之后这些村落发展为有数千人的 城镇。 后来这些城邦又发展为最早的拥

paragraphs.en: first human settlements to ever have more than 10,000 people. In other words, around 4,000 BC, at the end of the Ubaid period, we see the emergence of the first true cities in human history. This is the next period in Sumerian history. It is called the Uruk period because Uruk was the biggest of these new cities. But other cities like Umma, Eridu, Ur and Nippur also developed in the floodplains of Mesopotamia. Here is that bigger timeline again, and here you can see when the Jarmo, Ubaid and Uruk periods happened relative to the big ages of history.
paragraphs.zh: 有上万人口的人类群落。 换句话说，在大约公元前4,000年， 欧贝德时期末期，人类历史上最早 的真正意义上的城邦出现了。 这是苏美尔历史的下一个时期。 这个时期被称为乌鲁克时期，因为 乌鲁克是这些新兴城邦中规模最 大的。 然而其它一些城邦也在美索不达 米亚地区的洪积平原上发展起来， 比如乌马、埃里都、乌尔，和尼普 尔。 再来看看这条大的时间线，你可以 看到贾莫、欧贝德，和乌鲁克恰好 都与大的历史时代相关联。

paragraphs.en: As you can see, these three periods happened during the late Neolithic and also during the CopperAge. I will be including timelines like this in all future lessons to help you better understand when things are happening. Now, there is an interesting concept in sociology. This is called "Dunbar's Number". It was named in the 1990s by the BritishAnthropologist Robin Dunbar. Dunbar proposed that we human beings can only manage a limited number of stable relationships. He said that limit is about 150 people.
paragraphs.zh: 如你所见，这三个时期分别处在新 石器时代晚期，和铜器时代。 在未来的课程中，我都会设置这样 的时间线，来帮助大家更好地理解 历史事件发生的时间。 现在，我要给大家讲一个有趣的社 会学概念。 那就是“邓巴数字”。 它是在 20 世纪 90 年代由英国人类 学家罗宾·邓巴命名的。 邓巴提出人类只能维持有限的稳 定关系。 他表示这个上限就是大约 150 个 人。

paragraphs.en: This is the number of people you know personally and keep in touch with on a regular basis. Dunbar described this number as: "The number of people you would not feel embarrassed about joining uninvited for a drink, if you happened to bump into them in a bar." Or you can think about Dunbar's number as the maximum number of friendships that a human being can manage. When people were living as hunter-gatherers, or in early agricultural villages like Jarmo, the size of the society was about the size of Dunbar's number. This meant that society could be organized informally through direct
paragraphs.zh: 这表示的是你个人认识并且能保 持规律联系的人。 邓巴这样形容这个数字： “如果你在酒吧偶遇了这群人，你 并不会觉得不请自来地跟他们喝 一杯有何不妥。” 或者你也可以将邓巴数字看出一 个人能维持的朋友数量的上限。 当人类处于狩猎采集时期，或类似 贾莫的早期农业社会时，社会的规 模差不多正好符合邓巴数字。 这意味着社会可以通过成员间的 直接人际关系构建起来。

paragraphs.en: personal relationships. The leaders of the society would be the people who everyone in the group most respected. If there was a fight, friends who knew both parties could be asked to mediate the dispute. There is nothing strange about this. This is still how we manage our personal lives today. And originally society was small enough that it could be managed in this way as well. Even in towns of a few thousand people, things could still be managed with overlapping networks of relationships. But once you start living in a true city, with a population greater than
paragraphs.zh: 社会的领导者则由群体中的最受 敬重者担任。 如果发生冲突，冲突双方共同的朋 友可以居中调停。 这些都是理所当然的事。 如今我们还是在以这种方式处理 我们的个人生活。 而最初的社会非常小，也可以如此 管理。 即使是在有数千人的城镇，诸般事 宜仍然可以靠成员间交错重叠的 关系网来处理。 然而一旦你开始在一座人口过万 的真正的城邦生活，这种人员管理

paragraphs.en: 10,000, this way of managing humans becomes impossible. At that scale our minds become overwhelmed. Its just not possible to have a meaningful relationship with thousands of other people. In a city, the vast majority of people who you live with are going to be strangers. So how do you live together in peace and solve conflicts? How do you assign work or organize society? For example, in Uruk, the population may have been as large as 40,000 people. Truly, the first cities created a new challenge for human organization.
paragraphs.zh: 方式就难以应对了。 在这种规模下，我们的个人意志相 形见绌。 要与上千人保持实质性的关系根 本就是无稽之谈。 在一座城邦中，与你共同生活的绝 大多数人都是陌生人。 那么你们该如何和睦相处、解决冲 突呢？ 又该如何分配工作或组织社会呢? 举例来说，在乌鲁克，人口可能已 经达到了 4 万之众。 诚然，这些最早的城邦给人口组织 带来了新的挑战。

paragraphs.en: In Mesopotamia, the solution to this problem was religion. Each of these first cities would be organized around a temple that was dedicated to the worship of a different god. Religious festivals and feasts, and rituals created a sense of psychological unity among the people. The people of these first cities believed that their city was home to a specific god who lived in the temple. This god or goddess was the protector of the city, and would care for the people by sending good harvests and keeping them safe. In return the people would worship this god and make donations to the
paragraphs.zh: 在美索不达米亚地区，这个问题的 解决方案是宗教。 这些早期城邦全都是围绕某座神 庙构建起来的，这些神庙供奉着不 同的神祇。 宗教节日、庆典和仪式让人们在精 神上团结一心。 这些早期城邦的居民相信，他们的 城邦就是神庙中那位神明的家园。 神是城邦的守护者，并且赐予人们 良好的收成，以庇护他或她的子 民。 作为回馈，人们会供奉这尊神祇， 并向神庙献捐纳贡。

paragraphs.en: God at the temple. If you lived in one of these cities, you knew that your neighbor also worshipped the same god. This created a sense that you could trust your neighbor, since you all belonged to the same religion. It also meant that the temple, and the temple priests emerged as the government of these first cities. In every early civilization- Sumer, Egypt, China- the same thing seems to happen. Religion emerges as a system to create social cohesion once the society grows beyond a certain point. For example, in the city of Uruk, the people imported expensive stones and built a temple for the Goddess
paragraphs.zh: 如果你是这些城邦的居民，你心里 会清楚，你的邻居和你崇拜同样的 神明。 这令你对邻居产生信任，因为你们 都信奉同一种宗教。 这还意味着神庙，以及神庙中的祭 司成为了这些早期城邦的政府。 这一点在每个早期文明都是共通 的，苏美尔、古埃及、古代中国皆 不例外。 一旦社会规模达到某种程度，宗教 就成了凝聚社会的一种系统。 比如，在乌鲁克，人们买来名贵的 石材为伊南娜女神建造神庙。

paragraphs.en: Inanna. They believed that the goddess actually lived in the temple and protected Uruk as her home. So it was religion that first let the Sumerians manage a complex society. Please join me next time as we explore exactly how they did that. Now the lesson is over, but perhaps you'd like to learn more. From now on each of our lessons will end with a section where I recommend some additional resources, and I tell you where you can find some of the artifacts that I've talked about. The opening slide in this lesson shows you a picture of the Nebra
paragraphs.zh: 他们相信女神真的居住在神庙中， 庇护着她的家园乌鲁克。 所以在最初，正是宗教让苏美尔人 得以管理复杂的社会。 那么欢迎继续收听我的下一讲，我 们一起来探索他们是如何做到这 一点的。 这一讲到此结束，但是或许你还想 了解更多知识。 从这一讲开始，每堂课的结尾都会 增加一个版块。我会在这里推荐一 些补充材料，并且告诉你们我提到 的一些工艺品在哪里可以找到。 这一讲开篇的图片展示的是内布 拉星象盘。

paragraphs.en: Sky disk. This can be seen in the State Museum of Prehistory in Halle Germany. The picture of Sumerian writing comes from the British Museum in London. And the JarmoArtifacts can be found at the Oriental Institute at the University of Chicago. Jarmo village is an important archaeological dig site, that is located in Northern Iraq. And the picture of the goddess Inanna which I used in slide 14 comes from an artifact called the Burney Relief. Finally, if this period in history interests you, I'd like to recommend two great resources.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以在德国哈雷州立史前博物 馆见到它。 苏美尔文字的图片来自伦敦的大 英博物馆。 而贾莫的工艺品藏于芝加哥大学 东方研究所。 贾莫村落是一出重要的考古发掘 遗迹，位于伊拉克北部。 而我在第 14 张图片中展示的伊南 娜女神的图片来自于被称为伯尼 浮雕的工艺品。 最后，如果你对这段历史时期很感 兴趣，我想给你推荐两个非常棒的 补充资源。

paragraphs.en: The first is a podcast called "The Podcast History of Our World" by Rob Monaco. And the second is my all-time favorite history book: The Cartoon History of the Universe by Larry Gonick. Okay that's it for today. Thanks everyone, see you next time.
paragraphs.zh: 第一个是一档名为《世界历史播 客》的在线音频广播，由罗伯·摩 纳哥主讲。 第二个是我最最喜爱的一本历史 书：《漫画宇宙史》，作者拉里·戈 尼克。 好了，这就是今天的内容。 谢谢大家，再见。

titleZh: 第二讲：苏美尔诸神与酒

paragraphs.en: Last time we discussed 4000 years of Mesopotamian history, from about 7000 to 3000 BC. We talked about Jarmo, one of the first agricultural settlements in this part of the world. This period lasted from 7 to 5,000 BC. Then we discussed the Ubaid period and how the Mesopotamians came down out of the hills and began to live directly on the river valleys. This period lasted from 6500 to 3800 BC. Finally we discussed Uruk period, which lasted from 4000 - 3100 BC, and is named after the largest of these cities, Uruk, which had a population of about 40,000 people. Here are those 3 periods on our
paragraphs.zh: 上 节 课 我 们 讨 论 了 在 公 元 前 7000-3000 年间，长达 4000 多年 的美索不达米亚文明史。 我们讲到了在那里出现的最早的 农业定居点之一——贾莫，发生在 公元前 5000-7000 年之间。 我 们 接 下 来 讨 论 了 公 元 前 6500-3800 年间的欧贝德时期，并 且讲述了美索不达米亚人是如何 走出大山，开始在河谷定居的。 最后，我们讨论的是公元前 4000 年至公元前 3100 年间的乌鲁克时 期，乌鲁克是当时最大的城市，拥 有大约 4 万人口。 从时间线上我们可以看到这 3 个

paragraphs.en: timeline. If you were paying close attention to the dates you noticed that these three periods of history overlap, and that's because there is no sharp change that separates them, but rather a gradual transition from one period of Mesopotamian history into the next. The Mesopotamian society which developed during the Uruk period is a natural place to start the story of human history, because these were the first people in the world to live in cities and develop writing. Historians call this society which developed along the lower Mesopotamia "Sumer" and the people are called the "Sumerians." This is actually the name that other cultures gave these people.
paragraphs.zh: 时期。 如果仔细观察这些年份，我们会发 现三个时期间有重叠部分，这是因 为美索不达米亚文明史的不同阶 段并不是突变的，而是逐渐更迭 的。 乌鲁克时期的美索不达米亚社会 是人类历史的开端，因为这是世界 上最早出现人类居住在城市的时 期，也是最早出现文字的时期。 历史学家将这个沿着美索不达米 亚平原发展起来的社会称为“苏美 尔”，将这里的人们称为“苏美尔 人”。实际上，这个命名是别的文 化赋予他们的，而非苏美尔人自 称。

paragraphs.en: For example, In the Hebrew Bible book of Genesis, Chapter 10, Verse 10 that part of the world is called "The land of Shinar." In the laterAkkadian language, that region is called " Sumeru", which is why we call this culture "Sumer" today. But the Sumerians themselves did not use that term.As far as we can guess, they called themselves "Sag-Gig-Ga" which means "The Black-Headed People". However, since all the history textbooks use the word "Sumer" that is what I will be using too. Last time we also talked about Dunbar's number, and how it becomes impossible for human beings to have personal relationships with all of their
paragraphs.zh: 例如，在希伯来圣经《创世记》第 10 章第 10 节就曾将其描述为“士 拿地”。 在后来的阿卡德语中，这一区域被 称为“须弥”，这也是为什么如今 我们称其为“苏美尔”。 但是苏美尔人们并没有用过这些 称谓。我们可以猜测的是，他们曾 称自己为“塞格吉加”，意为“黑 头发的人”。 不过，既然现在所有的历史书都用 “苏美尔”一词，那我也会用这个 词。 上次我们还讲到了“邓巴数字”： 当定居点的人数多达数百人时，人 们不可能与每一个人都建立直接 社交关系。

paragraphs.en: neighbors once a settlement grows beyond a few hundred people. In cities, where the population is greater than 10,000, it becomes impossible for humans to organize along personal relationships any longer. Instead people had to find new systems that allowed them to live peaceful among thousands of strangers. In Mesopotamia, as well as in every other ancient society, the solution was religion. Each city built a temple dedicated to the worship of a god or goddess, and the people believed that this god protected them, guided them and actually lived in the city. For example, the city of Uruk worshipped the goddess, Innana who was the goddess of sex, love,
paragraphs.zh: 在人口数量超过 1 万的城市中，人 们不可能与每个人都熟识。相反， 人们必须去寻找一个新的、能够与 数千名陌生人和平共存的机制。 在美索不达米亚，在任何一个古代 社会，解决问题的办法都是：宗教。 每个城市修建一座神庙，神庙中供 奉神，人们相信神会保护他们、指 引他们，并且相信神就住在这座城 市。 例如，乌鲁克城供奉的神是女神伊 南娜，她是性神、爱神及战神，也 是金星的代表神。

paragraphs.en: war and was also associated with the planet Venus. But the other cities did the same thing. Ur worshipped Nanna. Nippur worshipped, Enlil. Sippar worshiped Utu. Lagash worshipped, Ninurta. and Eridu worshipped Enki. In each of these Sumerians cities, the worship of the city god created the means of social organization. And these gods represented each city. For example, when two cities fought each other, the history of that fight would often be described as a fight between the two gods of those cities. Many of the gods and goddesses were associated with different colors, powers and objects in the
paragraphs.zh: 其他的城市也是一样地供奉神祇。 乌尔城供奉南纳； 尼普尔城供奉恩利尔； 西巴尔城供奉乌图； 拉加什城供奉尼努尔塔； 埃里都城供奉恩基。 在这些苏美尔人的城市中，对城市 神的供奉提供了组织社会的手段。 神是城市的象征。比如，当两座城 市发起战争时，人们会说这是两个 城市神在战斗。 不同的神、女神有不同的代表色， 各具神力，并与夜空中不同的物体 相对应。

paragraphs.en: night sky. Anu was the chief god. He was the god of heaven and the equatorial sky. His color was red. Enlil was the god of the air and of the northern sky, his color was purple. Marduk was Jupiter. Ningirsu was the god of agriculture and Saturn. His color was black. Inanna was the goddess of sex and war. She was also Venus and her color was blue. Nergal was the god of fire, his color was red and he was also Mars. Nabu was the god of wisdom. His color was orange, and he was also Mercury. Nanna was the god of the moon, and
paragraphs.zh: 安努是主神。他是天神，赤道神， 代表色为红色。 恩基是水神，男性生育神，以及南 方天神，代表色为绿色。 马杜克是木星神 宁吉尔苏是农业神，土星神，代表 色是黑色。 伊南娜是性神及战神，代表色是蓝 色。 涅迦尔是火神，代表色是红色。他 也是火星神。 纳布是智慧神，代表色是橘色，他 也是水星之神。 南纳是月神，代表色是银色。

paragraphs.en: his color was silver. And finally, Utu was the god of the Sun and his color was gold. We get a sense of how the Sumerians thought about their gods when we read the Epic of Gilgamesh, one of the earliest works of world literature. The story describes the life of a mythical hero named Gilgamesh who lived during the Uruk period of Sumerian history. In this excerpt, the poem describes the city of Uruk with pride. Listen to what it has to say. Go up, pace out the walls of Uruk. Study the foundation terrace and examine the brickwork. Is not its masonry of kiln-fired brick? And did not seven masters lay its
paragraphs.zh: 最后，乌图，太阳神，代表色是金 色。 通过世界上最早的文学作品《吉尔 伽美什史诗》，我们可以了解到苏 美尔人是如何看待他们的神的。这 本书讲述了传奇英雄吉尔伽美什 一生的故事，故事背景是苏美尔文 明乌鲁克时期。 在这节摘录中，描写乌鲁克城的诗 句里充满了骄傲。 听，这不得不说的话。 向前走，走出乌鲁克的城墙。 细细看看这露台的地基，仔细瞧瞧 这砖块。 这不就是自家砖窑生产的砖块 吗？ 这难道不是七位大师联手造就的

paragraphs.en: foundation? One square mile of city, one square mile of gardens, One square mile of clay pits, a half square mile of Ishtar’s dwelling, three and a half square miles is the measure of Uruk! We can learn several things from this poem. The city of Uruk was 3 and half square miles in size. One square mile is gardens inside the city walls. One square mile is houses. One square mile is clay pits for the construction of bricks and building materials.And one and a half square miles belongs to the Temple. But notice the poem refers to the temple as "Ishtar's Dwelling." Before I go any further I should also mention another thing, which probably held the society together besides religion: beer!
paragraphs.zh: 吗？ 一平方英里是城区，一平方英里是 花园， 一平方英里是黏土坑，还有半平方 英里归伊什塔尔， 这三又半平方英里就是乌鲁克！ 我们从诗句中可以得到以下信息： 乌鲁克城占地 3.5 平方英里；城墙 内有占地 1 平方英里的花园；住宅 区占地 1 平方英里；用于制作砖块 等建筑材料的黏土坑占地 1 平方 英里，还有占地 0.5 平方英里的神 庙。诗句中将神庙表述为——伊什 塔尔的住所。 在我继续之前，我需要提一个除宗 教以外的社会粘合剂：啤酒！

paragraphs.en: We think the Sumerians were the first people in the world to make beer, sometime around 3900 BC. Drinking beer with friends and family, and getting drunk with them was probably also one of the things that made city life attractive for these ancient people, and it may have been one of the things that helped persuade hunter-gatherers to give up that lifestyle and come live in the city. In fact, the Epic of Gilgamesh also has something to say about beer in society. In the story there is a character named Enkidu. He is very strong and lives out in nature among the animals. In the poem, a temple priestess named Shamhat goes out into nature and tames Enkidu, making him
paragraphs.zh: 我们认为苏美尔人是世界上最早 酿造啤酒的人，这一事件大约发生 在公元前 3900 年。 邀三五亲朋，举杯畅饮，不醉不 归……这可能是城市生活对古代 人们的吸引力之一，这也有可能是 促使采集狩猎者放弃原有生活方 式，转而生活在城市的原因之一。 事实上，《吉尔伽美什史诗》中就 曾提到过啤酒。在故事中有一个名 为恩奇杜的角色。 他非常强壮， 生活在大自然中，与动物为伴。 在这首诗中，一位名叫夏哈特的神 庙女祭司走进大自然，驯服并教化 了恩奇杜。 而她引诱他远离大自

paragraphs.en: civilized. One of the ways that she entices him away from nature is with beer. This is what the poem has to say: But Enkidu knew nothing about these things, so he sat and stared at the cooked food and beer for a very long time, not knowing what to do. The Shamhat, the Temple priestess, said to him: "Enkidu, this is the food and drink that men eat and drink. Eat and drink your fill. So Enkidu ate his fill of the cooked food and drank the beer, Seven jugs of the beer. And he was suddenly joyful and sang aloud. The temple priestess Shamhat being the one who civilizes Enkidu is also
paragraphs.zh: 然的方式之一就是让他喝啤酒。 正如这首诗所说的： 恩奇杜对这些事情一窍不通，所以 他就坐在那儿，盯着那些煮好的食 物还有啤酒，盯了好久，不知道要 做些什么。 神庙女祭司夏哈特对他说：“恩奇 杜，这是男人们吃的食物，喝的酒， 请你吃饱喝足。” 七壶啤酒接连下肚。 他突然觉得心满意足，不由得高歌 一曲。 神庙女祭司夏哈特驯化恩奇杜的 故事也是一个帮助我们了解苏美

paragraphs.en: another interesting clue to how Sumerian society worked. Since these early Sumerian cities were held together by the worship of the same god, naturally it was the priests and the temple that provided the earliest government of these cities. One of the most important artifacts from this period in Sumerian history is called the Warka vase. Sadly, the original vase was stolen during theAmerican invasion of Iraq inApril of 2003, but luckily it was returned to the Iraq National Museum a few months later in June. It had been smashed into pieces during that time. Fortunately it was carefully restored and is now on display at the National Museum of Iraq.
paragraphs.zh: 尔社会运转的有趣线索。 由于这些早期的苏美尔城市的人 们通过供奉同一神而聚居在一起， 所以实际上，当时的神庙、祭司是 这些城市的最早的政府形式。 在苏美尔历史上，这一时期最重要 的艺术品之一是乌鲁克祭祀瓶。 很不幸，原花瓶在 2003 年 4 月— —美国发起伊拉克战争时被盗，但 幸运的是，数月后，花瓶于同年 6 月重回伊拉克国家博物馆。在被盗 期间，花瓶被打碎，但最终被精心 修复并在伊拉克国家博物馆展出。

paragraphs.en: The vase shows us people bringing offerings of fruit, grain, and animals to the goddess Inanna. People did this because they believed that such offerings would cause the goddess to bless them and bring them good fortune. In practice this meant that the temple-government collected large amounts of wealth from the people of Uruk, and the priests became some of the richest members of society. But the priests didn't just take the food and offering for themselves.At regular intervals they gave the food back to the people in the form of religious feasts. These religious festivals, done in honor of Inanna were key to maintaining social cohesion in the city.
paragraphs.zh: 从祭祀瓶中我们可以看到，人们将 水果、谷物、牲畜等祭祀品献给女 神伊南娜。人们之所以有此举动， 是因为他们相信这些祭祀品能够 让女神祝福他们并给他们带来好 运。 实际上，这意味着神庙-政府从乌 鲁克人民那里收集了大量财富，而 祭司也由此成为社会中最富有的 群体之一。 但祭司们并不是将这些祭祀品全 部占为己有，他们会定期以宗教节 日的形式将食物送回给人民，而这 些以伊南娜名义举办的宗教节日 是维持城市中社会凝聚力的关键。

paragraphs.en: The priests also maintained the storage of surplus food in the temple granaries. So if there was a bad harvest one year, and people were short on food, the temple would open the granaries and feed the people to prevent famine and social unrest. In this way the temple collected the surplus in the good years, and fed the people in the bad years, all through religious rituals which created social cohesion. The temple system was the glue which kept these first cities together and made society stable. However, the priests soon ran into another problem.As the temple became the part of society that stored and distributed the surplus food, they began needing a system
paragraphs.zh: 祭司们还将多余的食物保存在神 庙的粮仓中，当收成不好，人们缺 少食物的时候，神庙就会打开粮 仓，接济人民，从而避免饥荒与社 会动荡。 如此一来，神庙可以在收成好的时 候囤积余粮，年景不好时接济人 民，而这都是通过可以增强社会凝 聚力的宗教仪式而完成的。 神庙机制就是早期城市的粘合剂， 有效促进了社会稳定。 然而，祭司们很快遇到了另一个问 题。既然神庙承担着社会中贮存并 分发食物的职能，那么祭司们就需 要一套簿记系统。

paragraphs.en: for record keeping. Otherwise, how could you plan any of the religious festivals? As the cities grew in size, the records needed became bigger and more complex. Merchants also needed a system for record keeping and making contracts. So it was by solving these problems that the Sumerians became the first people in the world to develop writing. And that is what we will talk about next time. Okay everyone, that concludes the lecture. Now lets talk about ways you can learn more. First, the most important artifact mentioned in this lecture is probably the Warka Vase.As I mentioned in
paragraphs.zh: 否则，你如何才能安排宗教节日 呢？ 随着城市规模的扩张，记录体量越 来越大，愈发复杂了。 商人们也需要簿记系统，需要签订 合同。为了解决这些问题，苏美尔 人成为了世界上最早发明文字的 人类。 而这，就是我们下节课会讲到的内 容。 好了，本次课程就到这里，现在让 我们谈一谈想了解更多相关知识 时可以采取的办法。 首先，课堂上提到的最重要的艺术 品，可能非乌鲁克祭祀瓶莫属，我 也说过了，你可以在位于巴格达的

paragraphs.en: the lecture, this artifact can be found in the National Museum of Iraq in Baghdad. Tablets with the Epic of Gilgamesh and a seal cylinder showing Sumerians drinking beer can both be found in The British Museum in London. Finally, we've also mentioned the Epic of Gilgamesh a few times in this lesson. This is one of the oldest stories ever to be written down. So I suggest that you get yourself a copy and read it for yourself.
paragraphs.zh: 伊拉克国家博物馆一睹其风貌。 在伦敦大英博物馆，你可以找到刻 有吉尔伽美什史诗的泥板以及描 绘了苏美尔人喝啤酒场景的圆柱 封印。 最后，我们在这节课中多次提到了 《吉尔伽美什史诗》，这是有史以 来最古老的故事之一，我建议你们 自己去读一读。

paragraphs.en: Lecture Hello everyone and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will be learning about the origins of writing. The period we are discussing today covers over 4000 years, because the invention of writing was a gradual process that occurred over centuries between 7000 and 2500 BC. Here I show you how long that process took on our 12,000-year big timeline. Last class we learned how the Sumerian temples were the glue that held society together. During good years they collected the surplus food as offerings to the god.And they redistributed the food during religious feasts.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天，我们将学习文字的起源。 我们讨论的这段时期，跨度超过 4000 年。这是因为文字的发明过 程 持 续 数 千 年 ， 约 从 公 元 前 7000-2500年，是一个渐进的过程。 在这里，我要向大家展示，在持续 12,000 年的世界历史进程中，文 字的发明处于怎样的位置。 上节课我们讲到，苏美尔神庙是如 何将社会凝聚到一起的。 每临丰年，他们将剩余的食物作为 祭品献给神，还在宗教宴会上重新 分配食物。

paragraphs.en: In bad years they also opened the granaries and fed the people, preventing famines and social unrest. The temples managed a large portion of the society's surplus. But, this created a problem that the priests needed to solve. HOW could they keep records of all the goods that were coming into and leaving the temple store-rooms? Because without accurate records, it would be impossible to plan the redistribution of the food. The answer is that they first used a system which was as old as the Jarmo period. They used baked clay tokens to represent different goods. Here is a collection of tokens that
paragraphs.zh: 年景不好时，他们打开粮仓，养活 人民，避免饥荒和社会动荡。 社会盈余的很大一部分，都由神庙 管理分配。 但是，这就给祭司们出了一道难 题。他们怎么能把进出神庙储藏室 的所有物品都记录下来呢? 因为如果没有准确的记录，就不可 能计划重新分配食物。 答案是，他们率先使用了一种和贾 莫时期一样古老的系统。 他们用烘烤过的粘土标记来代表 不同的物品。 这是在苏萨城发现的一批标记。

paragraphs.en: was found in the city of Susa. Susa was not a Sumerian city. It belonged to a different people called the Elamites. The Elamites lived up in the Zagros hills instead of down in the river valleys like the Sumerians. But the Elamites were neighbors to the Sumerians and traded with them all the time. So we are confident that they used similar systems of record keeping. Look carefully at these clay tokens. Each one represents a different thing. Scholars have worked for a long time trying to understand the meaning of these tokens. Unfortunately, many of them are still mysteries, but we have been able to figure out the meaning of at
paragraphs.zh: 苏萨并不是苏美尔人的城市，它属 于另一个民族：埃兰。埃兰人居住 于扎格罗斯山脉，而不像苏美尔人 那样住在河谷里。 但埃兰人是苏美尔人的邻居，且常 与他们交易。因此我们相信，他们 使用了类似的记录保存系统。 请仔细看这些粘土标记，每一件都 代表了不同的物品。 学者们花了很长时间，试图理解这 些标记的含义。不幸的是，其中许 多仍是谜，但我们至少已经弄明白 其中一些的含义。

paragraphs.en: least a few of them. Here is a key to the meaning of some of these tokens. The sphere's and cones seem to be number markers. The flat disks are used to indicate wool and sheep. Afew of them indicate containers, maybe for liquid goods like milk or olive oil, while others indicate animals like dogs, cows or lions. As I mentioned before this token system was much older than Sumerian society. In fact, there is some evidence that this token system of record keeping goes back at least as far as 8000 BC. As I mentioned in the first lesson, communities like Jarmo traded for items like obsidian and shells that came from hundreds of kilometers
paragraphs.zh: 这是了解一些标记含义的关键所 在。 球体和圆锥形可能是数字标记。扁 平圆盘用来表示羊毛和绵羊。 其中有一些表示容器，可能用于存 放液态物品，比如牛奶或橄榄油， 而另一些则表示动物如狗、牛或狮 子。 正如我之前提到的，这个标记系统 比苏美尔社会要古老得多。事实 上，有一些证据表明，这种象征性 的记录保存系统至少可以追溯到 公元前 8000 年。 正如我在第一课中提到的，像贾莫 这样的社区用来自几百公里外的 黑曜石和贝壳来交换物品。也许这 些粘土标记可以让人们保存长途

paragraphs.en: away. Perhaps these tokens allowed people to keep records necessary for such long-distance trade. In any case these tokens are found all-across the Middle East as you can see from this map. Each black dot indicates a place where archaeologists have discovered this type of trade token. They are found as far west as southernAnatolia and as far east as the shores of the Caspian Sea. This is strong evidence that this entire region was connected with by networks. The tokens were also used by two merchants who wanted to make a contract for the future. The two merchants would take the tokens to be traded and place them
paragraphs.zh: 贸易所需的记录。 总之，你可以从这张地图上看到， 这些标记遍布整个中东地区。每一 个黑点都代表着考古学家发现这 类商品交易的地方。 西至安纳托利亚南部，东至里海沿 岸。 这有力地证明了整个区域都互相 关联。 两个想要签合同的商人，也会使用 这些符号。 这两个商人会把这些用于交易的 标记放在一个中空的粘土球里。考

paragraphs.en: inside a hollow clay sphere. Archaeologists call one of these spheres a "bulla", and they are basically envelopes. Then each merchant would stamp the sphere with his seal; this served as his signature. The clay ball would then be baked to make it hard. When it came time to fulfill the contract, the merchants would crack open the ball and count the tokens inside. Then both sides could ensure that the contract was fulfilled. This form of contract making and record keeping probably served as the basis for middle eastern trade from at least 6500 to 3500 BC. But as society's grew in complexity, and people needed to keep track of larger and larger numbers of goods,
paragraphs.zh: 古 学 家 将 其 中 一 个 球 命 名 为 “Bulla”，功能相当于信封。 然后双方会在球上盖章，其作用相 当于签名。 然后他们会烘烤这个粘土球，使其 变硬。当需要履行合同的时候，商 人们会把球打开，清点里面的粘土 标记。然后双方都可以确保合同的 履行。 至少在公元前 6500-3500 年间，这 种形式的合同制作和记录保存很 有可能就是中东贸易的基础。 但是随着社会越来越复杂，人们需 要追踪的商品也越来越多，一个苏 美尔记录制作者对此有了进一步

paragraphs.en: a Sumerian record keeper had an insight. Why bother putting the tokens inside the clay ball in the first place. Instead the tokens to be traded could just be pressed into the clay and tick marks could be made to indicate the amount. Then the two merchants could impress their seals and bake the tablet and this would work just as well. Next, somebody, (perhaps a Sumerian temple scribe) realized that the system could get even more efficient. Why bother with impressing the tokens into the clay if you could just draw the shapes instead. And so people began drawing images into clay to indicate different objects. This was the very
paragraphs.zh: 的思考。 首先，为什么要把这些标记放在泥 球里呢？要交易的标记可以直接 印在粘土上，用刻度标记来表示数 量。随后他们可以在上面盖章，烘 烤成陶板，就可以使用了。 接下来，有人（也许是苏美尔人的 神庙抄写员）意识到这个系统可以 变得更有效率。 既然可以直接把形状画出来，又为 什么还要再把标记印到粘土上 呢？ 从此，人们开始把图形画在粘土上 来表示不同的物品。这就是文字的 开端。

paragraphs.en: first writing. Now if you have to draw pictures all day long, you are going to look for ways to draw the picture faster and faster. So the Sumerian scribes developed a system of drawing each symbol using the fewest strokes possible. This simplified picture system developed around 3200 BC. But when you write in clay there is a problem. The Sumerians at first wrote from top to bottom and right to left. But this way of writing meant that they were more likely to accidentally smudge their previous work. So around 3000 BC, some of the scribes began writing from left to right. Now the scribes were less likely to
paragraphs.zh: 那如果你必须整天画画，你就会想 办法更快速地画出图画。 因此，苏美尔抄写员发明了一种用 最少的笔画来绘制每个符号的系 统。这个简化的图片系统在公元前 3200 年左右发展了起来。 但是当你用粘土写字的时候，就会 产生一个问题。苏美尔人写字，最 初采用从上到下，从右到左的方 式。但这种写作方式意味着他们很 有可能不小心把之前的作品弄脏。 大约在公元前 3000 年，一些抄写 员开始从左到右写作。这样一来， 抄写员就不太会不小心弄脏他们 的作品了。

paragraphs.en: smudge their work accidentally. But this was harder for the priests of the temple to read, since they had always been used to reading from top to bottom. So the scribes just rotated all of their symbols by 90 degrees. That way they could keep writing from left to right and the priests could keep reading from top to bottom. Then over the next 600 years, the symbols began to become more and more simplified. The scribes got rid of curves (which are hard to draw) and made all of their images into a collection of lines. Eventually all the words were a collection of these wedge-shaped lines called "cuneiform", which means "wedge shaped" in Latin.
paragraphs.zh: 但这就给神庙祭司造成了阅读困 难，因为他们已经习惯了从上往下 的阅读方式。 所以抄写员把所有的符号都旋转 了 90 度。这样他们就可以继续从 左到右写作，祭司可以从上至下阅 读。 在接下来的 600 年间，这些标记越 来越简化。抄写员去掉了曲线（不 好画），把所有图形都变成了直线 的组合。 最终，这些文字都由楔形线条组 成， 被命 名为 “ 楔形 文字 ”。 “cuneiform”在拉丁语中的意思 是“楔形的”。

paragraphs.en: Here is another illustration of this development. First were tokens, then pictures of the tokens, then the 90˚ rotation, then abstraction into lines, and finally complete abstraction. Here is a picture of what mature cuneiform writing looks like. Of course a writing system is not very useful if it is only a list of nouns. But the problem with language is that some words express ideas that are impossible to draw. It’s easy enough to draw a picture of a concrete noun like a "cow" or a "tree" or a "man". But how do you draw a more complicated idea like "life"? The answer to this problem came
paragraphs.zh: 这是文字发展的另一个例证。 首先是粘土标记，然后是标记的图 片，随后 90°旋转，再简化成线 条，最终抽象成完全的象征标记。 这是一张成熟楔形文字的图片。 当然，如果一个文字系统只是一个 名词列表，那就不是很实用了。 但语言的问题在于，很多词表达的 想法很难用绘画的方式表现出来。 画一个像“牛”或“树”或“人” 这样的具体名词的图片很容易。但 是你如何画出一个更复杂的概念， 比如“生命”？ 这个问题的答案来自于苏美尔语

paragraphs.en: from the nature of the Sumerian language. You see Sumerian, like Chinese, has a lot of homophones, that is words that sounds like each other. For example, the Sumerian word for life was "til" The Sumerian word for "ti"。 "arrow" is Since “ti”sounds like “til”,the Sumerians just used the symbol for arrow whenever they wanted to express the word “life”. By 2400 BC, the Sumerian system had developed into a full writing system. By then they used a combination of different wedge shapes to express every different syllable sound in the language. Linguists call this sort of writing system a syllabary.
paragraphs.zh: 言的特质。苏美尔语和中文一样， 有很多同音异形异义词，就是听起 来很像的词。 比如，苏美尔语中表示“生命”的 词是“til”，而表示“箭头”的词 是“ti”。 由于“ti”和“til”听起来很像， 他们会用表示箭头的符号来表示 生命。 到公元前 2400 年，苏美尔语言系 统已经发展出一套完整的文字体 系。那时，他们用楔形形状来表达 口语中不同的音节。 语言学家把这种文字体系称为音 节文字。

paragraphs.en: And since the writing system could express sounds, they could use it to write every word in the Sumerian language. And even better, since all of this was written in Clay, and clay lasts for a long time, we have a huge number of records from ancient Sumeria.And with these we can begin to tell real history. So please join me next time.We will look at some of these ancient writings and i will tell you the story of the first war in recorded history, the first kings, the first social revolution, and the first empire. Ok, that concludes the lecture, but I hope some of you are eager to learn more about Cuneiform and the history of writing. If you want to see some of these
paragraphs.zh: 由于这种文字体系可以表达口语 中任意一个音节，它可以用来表达 苏美尔语中的所有单词。 更棒的是，因为所有这些都写在粘 土上，而粘土可以留存很长一段时 间，我们拥有了大量古代苏美尔的 记录。有了这些，我们就可以开始 讲述真实的历史了。 下节课，请和我一起继续学习世界 历史。我们会进一步了解古代文 字，我会向你们讲述有史以来的第 一场战争，第一代国王，第一次社 会革命，以及第一个帝国。 这节课结束了，但我希望还有同学 非常想要了解更多关于楔形文字 和文字的历史。 如果你想看看这些艺术品，纽约大

paragraphs.en: artifacts, the New York Metropolitan Museum ofArt, the British Museum and the Louvre all have excellent Cuneiform and Sumerian exhibits. But some of the best examples of the token writing system are found at the Louvre in Paris France. Another excellent resource are two Youtube channels which I highly recommend. First is Extra Credit's "The History of Writing", as you can probably guess some of my slides make use of illustrations from their video. They do a fantastic job illustrating the development of Cuneiform writing. The second wonderful channel is Matt and Tom, particularly the long episode where they learn about
paragraphs.zh: 都会艺术博物馆、大英博物馆和卢 浮宫都有极好的楔形文字和苏美 尔展品。 但是，抽象文字系统的最佳范例， 目前正在巴黎卢浮宫展出。 我还想向你们强烈推荐两个油管 频道。 第一个是 Extra Credit 的"The History of Writing"，我部分幻 灯片的插图就 使用了他们视频中 的截图。他们在展示楔形文字发展 方面做了了不起的工作。 另一个很好的渠道是 Matt and Tom，尤其是他们在向世界顶尖专 家欧文·芬克尔学习楔形文字的这

paragraphs.en: Cuneiform from Irving Finkel, one of the world's leading experts on the topic. Finally, I recommend these websites as very very rich sources of information. They are very academic and are probably quite difficult for most students, but they are excellent resources. All right, everyone. That concludes our lesson and I’ll see you next time.
paragraphs.zh: 一段。 最后，我推荐的这些网站是非常丰 富的信息来源。它们很学术，对大 多数学生来说可能相当困难，但确 是非常优质的资源。 好啦，今天的课就到这里，我们下 节课再见。

titleZh: 第四讲：4000 多年前发生在中东的“鹬蚌相争，渔翁得利”

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone, and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we are going to be learning about the first war in recorded history. This conflict took place between 2500 and 2400 BC. Here is where it would be on our timeline. So still early in the Bronze Age. Anyway, by the middle of the 3rd Millennium BC, the Sumerians had developed a mature writing system, capable of expressing every word in their language. And even better for historians, they wrote everything in clay, which is pretty much indestructible. So thousands of Sumerian records have survived to the present day, giving
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。今天我们将学习有记载以来的 第一场战争。 这 场 冲 突 发 生 在 公 元 前 2500-2400 年之间。这是它在时间 线上的位置，位于青铜时代早期。 总之，到了公元前 3000 年中期， 苏美尔人已经发明了一种成熟的 文字系统，能够表达他们语言中的 每一个词。 更令历史学家高兴的是，他们把一 切都写在粘土上，而粘土不容易随 时间流逝而损坏。因此，成千上万 苏美尔人的记录活到了今天，给了 我们一扇通往他们生活、他们的文

paragraphs.en: us a window into their lives, their culture and their politics. And with writing we begin to have proper history. And so what does that writing tell us? It tells us a story about a war and revolution. The very first in the historical record. The records that I'm going to tell you about today come from the next period in Sumerian history. This period is called the Early Dynastic Period, and it lasted from about 2900 to 2350 BC. If you remember our last classes, the previous period in Sumerian history was called the "Uruk Period." That lasted from 4000 to 2900 BC. We learned that during the Uruk
paragraphs.zh: 化和政治的窗口。 有了文字，我们就有了有迹可循的 历史。 那么，这些文字告诉我们什么？它 给我们讲了一个关于战争和革命 的故事。这是有历史记录以来的第 一场战争。 这些记录来自于苏美尔的下一个 时期。这个阶段被称为“早王朝时 期”，从公元前 2900 年持续到公元 前 2350 年。 如果你还记得我们的前几节课，公 元前 4000-2900 年，苏美尔历史上 一段被称为“乌鲁克”的时期。 我们了解到，在乌鲁克时期，神庙

paragraphs.en: period, the Temples and the Priests formed the government of the Sumerian city-states. However, this system began to change during the Early Dynastic period.At this time powerful men and military leaders began to gather more and more power for themselves. Probably they got this power during a war, and then never gave it up once the war ended. These men were called "Lu - Gal" in Sumerian, which means "Great Man." These men were the first Kings in history. Today we are going to learn about some of these kings.And we will start with this artifact: The Stele of Vultures. AStele is an upright stone slab that records some important message.
paragraphs.zh: 和祭司组成了苏美尔城邦的政府。 但是，这个体系在早王朝时期开始 发生了变化。在这个时期，有权势 的人和军事领导人开始将权力越 来越多地集中在自己手里。也许他 们是在战争中获得了这种权力，在 战争结束后，他们也不愿意放弃权 力。 这些人在苏美尔语中被称为“卢- 戈尔”，意思是“伟大的人”。而这 些人就是历史上第一批国王。 今天我们将要了解其中的几位国 王。我们将从这件工艺品开始讲 起：秃鹫石碑。 石碑是一块竖直放置的石板，上面 记录着重要信息。通常石碑的顶部

paragraphs.en: Oftentimes the top of the stele is curved. Chinese rulers often used Stele to record important messages, and a gravestone is also a type of Stele. Anyway in the 1880s, French Archaeologists were digging in Iraq when they discovered 4 fragments of a stele. This is one of the first artifacts to be discovered when historians were just beginning to learn about the Sumerians. This Stele is more than 4000 years old, and during that time it got broken into pieces. So we've only found 6 fragments, which as you can see, is only about 1/3rd of the original message. But we can read enough of the Cuneiform message to learn an interesting story. That is the story of
paragraphs.zh: 呈弯曲状。中国的统治者也经常用 石碑来记录重要信息，而墓碑也是 石碑的一种。 19 世纪 80 年代，法国考古学家在 伊拉克挖掘时发现了 4 个石碑碎 片。这是历史学家们刚开始了解苏 美尔文明时，最先接触到的古代文 物。 这块石碑已经有 4000 多年的历史 了，并在这段时间里逐渐分崩离 析。所以我们只发现了 6 块碎片， 如你所见，原始信息只有 1/3 保留 了下来。 但我们能够识别的楔形文字已经 富含了足够的信息量，让我们来了 解到这个有趣的故事：有史以来的

paragraphs.en: the first war in recorded history. And as you can see, we call this the Stele of Vultures, because the top-most piece has a carved image of vultures eating the body parts of soldiers who died in battle. Now, let me tell you the story of that war. The war was fought by two Sumerian cities: Lagash and Umma. In between both cities was a section of the Euphrates river that included rich soil, plenty of water, and a useful canal. This area was called the Plain of Gu-edina. Both cities wanted that land.And they could not agree who should have it. People fight over water and land today, and they fought over water
paragraphs.zh: 第一场战争。 如你所见，我们把这块石碑称为秃 鹫石碑，是因为它顶部雕刻了秃鹫 正在吃死在战场上士兵的场面。 现在，让我来向你讲述这场战争的 故事。 战争双方是两个苏美尔城邦：拉加 什和乌玛。这两个城邦的交界处， 有一片肥沃的土地，这里是幼发拉 底河的一部分，有着充足的水资源 和一条实用的运河。这片区域被称 为古埃迪那平原。 两个城邦都想要这片土地，双方无 法达成一致。 历史总是惊人地相似。今天，人们 为水资源和土地而战，4500 年前，

paragraphs.en: and land 4500 years ago. The more things change, the more they stay the same. So the two cities tried to settle the disagreement peacefully at first. Both cities respected the King of Kish, because in Sumerian mythology, Kish is the oldest city in the world. So the King of Kish came and investigated the situation. Then he put up a boundary marker in-between Umma and Lagash. In his decision he favored Lagash and gave them control of the canal. Interestingly, this canal still exists today! Nowadays, it is called the Al-Gharraf canal and it connects the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. The people of Iraq still use this very
paragraphs.zh: 人们同样为水和土地而战。 起初，这两个城邦试图和平解决争 端。两个城邦都尊敬基什国王，因 为在苏美尔神话中，基什是世界上 最古老的城邦。 于是基什国王前来察看情况，并在 拉加什和乌玛中间立了界碑。 他支持拉加什，并让他们控制运 河。 有趣的是，这条运河至今仍然存 在！如今，它被称为阿尔-加拉夫 运河，连接底格里斯河和幼发拉底 河。 伊拉克人民至今仍用这条运河来

paragraphs.en: canal for transportation. Anyway, as you can imagine, Enakali, the King of Umma was very unhappy about this decision. So after the King of Kish went home, Umma went out with his army, tore down the border marker, and moved it to the other side of the canal, claiming as his territory. Of course, the people of Lagash were not happy about this aggression by Umma. But for a generation they did nothing. But then about 50 years later, Lagash had a new King named E-ah-nah-tum. Eannatum was aggressive and willing to start a war for his city. So he marched out of Lagash at the head of his army, and Enakali also
paragraphs.zh: 运输。 想必你也能猜到，乌玛国国王，安 纳卡利对这个决定很不满。 于是，基什国王回国后，安纳卡利 率领大军，把界碑移到了运河另一 边，将运河纳入自己的版图。 当然，拉加什人民对这场侵略非常 不满，但当时，他们并没有做出反 应。 然而 50 年后，拉加什的新国王安 纳吐姆上任了。安纳吐姆是一个很 有侵略性的人，他愿意为城邦而 战。 因此他率领军队冲出城邦，而安纳 卡利也立于其阵首。

paragraphs.en: marched out with his army. This battle between Lagash and Umma is the battle recorded in the Stele of Vultures. Humans have probably been fighting each other for millions of years, even while we were still apes. After all, our closest relatives, Chimpanzees, frequently fight, and sometimes kill each other, so it is quite reasonable to assume that our ancestors behaved the same way. Violence between humans is probably older than fire or stone tools. But this battle between Lagash and Umma is significant, because it is the first battle with a written record. In a sense, the history of warfare
paragraphs.zh: 这场拉加什与乌玛之间的战争被 记录在秃鹫石碑上。 人类从还是猿的时候开始，就已经 有过数百万年的战斗历史了。 毕竟，我们最亲近的亲戚：黑猩猩， 就经常打架，有时还互相残杀。因 此我们有理由认为，祖先的行为有 其相似之处。 人与人之间的暴力，其历史可能比 火和石器工具更为久远。 但拉加什和乌玛之间的这场战役 意义重大，因为这是第一场有书面 记录的战争。 从某种意义上说，战争的历史从这

paragraphs.en: begins here, on the plain of Gu-Edina. The historical record tells us that King Eannatum led his army to victory and Lagash defeated Umma. But as so often happens in history, one act of violence leads to another as people seek revenge for past humiliation. And so we believe that Umma and Lagash probably fought each other again and again for the next 50 years. But in the end, neither city won the war! Both countries wasted their money and soldiers fighting each other. After decades of war and dozens of battle, both of them were badly weakened.
paragraphs.zh: 里，遥远的古埃迪那平原，开始了。 历史记载告诉我们，安纳吐姆带领 他的军队走向了胜利，拉加什打败 了乌玛。 冤冤相报何时了，人们会为过去所 受的屈辱复仇。 因此我们相信乌玛和拉加什很可 能会在接下来的 50 年里反复争 斗。 然而，最后这两个城邦都没能赢得 这场战争。他们在战争中白白牺牲 了钱财与将士。 经过几十年的战争和几十场战争， 双方实力都严重受损。

paragraphs.en: So their neighbor in the north, the King of Zabalam, saw an opportunity. He attacked Umma and defeated their weakened army, conquering the city and making it part of his Kingdom.And then he defeated the army of Lagash and pushed them all the way back to their own city. After that he took the canal for himself. So neither Umma nor Lagash won the war, in the end they both lost. When I was reading about this war, I was reminded of a story from Chinese history. The story is the origin of the Chengyu: Yu Bang Xiang Zheng, Yu Ren De Li. This story is from the Warring
paragraphs.zh: 他们北方的邻居，撒巴兰国王，看 到了机会。 他进攻乌玛，战胜了他们虚弱的军 队，征服了这座城邦，将其纳入自 己的版图。随后他又打败了拉加 什，将其逼退回城邦境内。 随即，他占领了运河。 鹬蚌相争，渔人得利。乌玛和拉加 什双双败北。 当我读到这场战争的时候，我想起 了一个中国的历史故事。有一个成 语便来自于这个故事： 鹬蚌相争，渔人得利。 这个故事来自中国历史上的战国

paragraphs.en: States period in Chinese history. In the story the King of Zhao, Hui Wang, wants to attack the state of Yan. But his advisor, Su Dai, tells him a story about a sandpiper and a clam that got into a fight. Neither one could win the fight, and they were both caught by a fisherman. The story is an allegory for the danger that the state of Qin might conquer both Zhao and Yan if they fight each other. But the story could work just as well for describing the war between Umma and Lagash. The Stele of the Vultures describes a war that happened 2200 years before the Warring States period in China.And yet the situation is basically the same. This is why we should study
paragraphs.zh: 时期。赵惠王想要攻打燕国，辩士 苏代给他讲了一个故事：一只鹬和 一只蚌互相搏斗，双方都不可能赢 得这场战斗，它们都被一个渔民抓 住了。 这个故事是一则民间寓言，说明如 果燕赵互相争斗，秦国可能会战胜 赵和燕。 但这个故事也可以用来描述乌玛 和拉加什之间的战争。 秃鹫石碑描述的这场战争，比中国 的战国时期要早了 2200 年。然而 情况基本上是相同的。 这就是为什么我们应该学习历史。

paragraphs.en: history. Human nature is fundamentally the same in every country and every time period. So the mistakes and lessons and stories from any country's history is useful for understanding people, and every historical story has useful lessons to teach us, if we have the wisdom to think and listen. We are all human beings, so world history is our common heritage, not just the history of our own nation. So I hope you will continue to listen to these stories in our class and learn some wisdom. Please join me next time as we find out what happened to Umma and Lagash in the century after their war. Okay everyone, that concludes the
paragraphs.zh: 在任何国家和任何一个时期，人的 本性都是一样的。 因此，任何国家历史上的错误、教 训和故事都有助于我们理解人性。 如果我们懂得倾听和思考，我们就 可以从每个历史故事中获得经验 教训。 我们都是人，所以世界历史是全人 类的共同遗产，而不应仅仅关注我 们自己国家的历史。 因此我希望你们能够继续从我们 的世界历史课程中了解到这些故 事，并从中获取智慧。 下节课请和我一起来看看乌玛和 拉加什在战后的一个世纪里发生 了什么。 课程到此结束，但我希望你们能学

paragraphs.en: lecture. I hope you would like to learn more, so here come some study resources. First, you should know that the war between Lagash and Umma took place in a part of the world that is now southern Iraq.And the canal they fought over still exists! Its called theAl-Gharraf canal. The primary artifact that gave us today's story is the Stele of Vultures. You could go see the original stele for yourself if you go to Paris because it is on display in the Louvre Museum. And finally these two websites have excellent details on both the stele and the battle. One of them is the Louvre website itself. So if you want to learn more you can go
paragraphs.zh: 到更多，所以这里有一些学习资 源。 首先，你应该知道，拉加什和乌玛 之间的战争发生在现在的伊拉克 南部地区。他们争夺的运河仍然存 在！它叫做阿尔-加拉夫运河。 这节课提到的重要文物是秃鹫石 碑。 如果有机会去巴黎，你可以亲自去 卢浮宫参观这座石碑。 最后，这两个网站上有关于这场战 役的详细信息。其中一个是卢浮宫 官网。如果你想了解更多信息，可 以登录查看。

paragraphs.en: check it out there. Okay everyone, thank you very much. See you next time!
paragraphs.zh: 谢谢大家，下节课再见！

titleZh: 第五讲：史上第一个帝国的诞生与萨尔贡王传奇

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone, and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Last time I told you the story of the oldest war in the historical record. Today I will tell you about the oldest social revolution. This story also takes place in the city of Lagash. The story I'm going to tell you today takes place over about 50 years, from 2330 BC until 2279 BC. Here is what it looks like on our timeline. Our story begins about 2 generations after the war that I told you about last class. At the end of last class, King Il of Zabalam had conquered Umma and
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。上节课我们学习了有史以来最 古老的战争，今天我们将学习历史 上第一次社会革命。 这个故事发生在拉加什城邦。 今天我要讲的故事，前后持续约 50 年，发生在公元前 2330-2279 年间。这是它在时间线上的位置。 这个故事的开端，发生于上次我们 所讲述的第一场战争结束后两代 人的时间里。 上节课结束时，扎巴拉姆二世征服 了乌玛，建立了一个统治着多个城

paragraphs.en: built himself a large kingdom which ruled over many different cities. But his sons were not as strong as he was, and after his death, his Kingdom fell apart and the city of Umma regained its independence. And of course, Umma and Lagash went back to fighting each other again. In the year 2320 BC, Lagash was ruled by King Lugalanda and Queen Baranamtarra. This King and Queen of Lagash were ambitious and loved luxury. They wanted to build up the army of Lagash to make it the most powerful in Sumeria, and they also wanted to live lives of extreme luxury. But of course, a powerful army, and
paragraphs.zh: 邦的大王国。但他的儿子们并不像 他那么能干，在他死后，他的王国 分崩离析，乌玛城重新获得了独 立。 当然，乌玛和拉加什又开始互相争 斗了。 公元前 2320 年，拉加什由国王卢 伽尔兰达和王后巴拉南塔拉统治。 这两人野心勃勃，喜爱奢华。想要 将拉加什的军队打造成苏美尔地 区最强大的军事力量，并让自己过 上穷奢极欲的生活。 但是，当然，强大的军队和奢华的

paragraphs.en: a luxurious life need to be paid for. So King Lugalanda made the taxes in Lagash very, very high. We know all of this happened because of this cone-shaped piece of clay. There is Cuneiform writing inscribed all around it, and it tells us the story of Lagash at that time: "In those days, the boat inspector stole boats from the people. The livestock official stole donkeys and sheep. The fisheries official stole fish. The King stole the animals and lands that belong to the temple. In the garden of a poor man, an official could cut down a tree or steal his fruit. When a man died a heavy tax was collected. From one end of the land to the other, everywhere there
paragraphs.zh: 生活价值不菲。因此，卢伽尔兰达 在拉加什的税收非常非常高。 我们能够了解到这些信息，完全归 功于这个圆锥形的粘土块。它上面 雕刻着楔形文字，向我们讲述了当 时拉加什的故事： 在那些日子里，船只检察官从人民 手中窃取船只。 牲畜官窃取驴羊。 渔官窃鱼。 国王窃走属于神庙的动物和土地。 官员走进穷人的花园，砍倒他的他 的树木，偷走他的果实。 一个人倒下了，国家就能收一笔 税。 国家的每一个角落，都遍布着税收 官。”

paragraphs.en: was the tax collector.” For many years, this was the situation in Lagash. The taxes were heavy. The rich took advantage of the poor. The King and his official were corrupt.And if a family could not afford to pay their taxes, they could be sold into slavery. But eventually the people of Lagash had enough, and they rebelled. They overthrew their King and Queen and they elected a new King named Urukagina. This is the first social revolution in recorded history and Urukagina is history's first reformer. Once Urukagina became King he made many reforms. He fired the corrupt officials.
paragraphs.zh: 这样的情况在拉加什持续了许多 年。横征暴敛，高层腐败，如果一 个家庭不能负担赋税，就会被迫成 为奴隶。 最终，拉加什的人民受够了，他们 反叛了。 他们推翻了他们的国王和王后，选 出了一个新的国王：乌鲁卡基纳。 这是历史上第一次社会革命，乌鲁 卡基纳是历史上第一个改革家。 乌鲁卡基纳成为国王后，他做出了 许多改革措施。 他解雇了那些腐败的官员。

paragraphs.en: He protected the gardens of the poor from the rich. He cancelled the debts of indentured families. He made it so that the poor could not be forced to sell to the rich at unfair prices. He lowered taxes, and he passed laws protecting widows and orphans. For 8 years, things went well in Lagash. Urukagina was very popular, and the society became more fair and just. Unfortunately, there was a downside to lowering taxes. This made life easier for the poor, BUT it also meant that the government had less money. With less tax revenue coming in,
paragraphs.zh: 他保护穷人的利益，使其不再遭受 富人的剥削。 他取消了契约家庭的债务。 他这样做，是为了让穷人不再以低 廉的价格被卖给富人。 他降低了税收，还新建法律来保护 孤儿和寡妇。 八年来，一切进展顺利。乌鲁卡基 纳很受欢迎，社会变得更加公平公 正。 但是，降低税收也有其不利的一 面。穷人的生活虽然得到了改善， 国家税收却因此减少了。 随着税收收入的减少，乌鲁卡基纳

paragraphs.en: Urukagina did not have as much money to pay for the army, and so Lagash became weaker. After 8 years of this, Zagessi, the King of Umma saw an opportunity and he attacked Lagash. He defeated their weakened army and Urukagina was overthrown. As he lost power, Urukagina pronounced a curse on King Zagesi: And as for King Zagessi, may his goddess place his mortal sin upon his head. As it turns out, Urukagina's curse would eventually come true, BUT not for many years. Zagessi conquered all of the city-states in southern Sumer, and ruled a great Kingdom for the next 25 years.
paragraphs.zh: 用于供养军队的资金就变少了，拉 加什的军事力量随之减弱。 减税 8 年后，乌玛国国王扎格西找 到了机会，率军攻打拉格什。他大 获全胜，推翻了乌鲁卡基纳的统 治。 乌鲁卡基纳失去权力后，对扎格西 施加了诅咒： 至于扎格西国王，愿他的女神将死 罪降临在他的头上。 事实证明，乌鲁卡基纳的诅咒最终 会成为现实，但这是很多年以后的 事情了。 扎格西征服了苏美尔南部的所有 城邦，并在接下来的 25 年里统治 了一个伟大的王国。

paragraphs.en: Here is a map of Zagessi's territory. The red represents his territory, and the yellow represents the land of Elam, the neighbors of Sumer who had their capital city at Susa. Now do you see that green circle around the city ofAkkad? That is where the next character in our story will be coming from. But first I need to tell you about a change that was slowly happening in Mesopotamia. The people who first built the great cities like Uruk, Kish, Umma, Lagash and Ur were the Sumerians; they spoke the Sumerian language. But during the 3rd Millennium BC, a new people began moving into this part of the world and living with the Sumerians.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一张扎格西统治疆域的地图。 红色代表他的领土，黄色代表埃 兰。埃兰是苏美尔的邻邦，都城为 苏萨。 你看到阿卡德周围的绿色圈圈了 吗？我们下一个故事的主角就来 自这里。 但首先我要告诉你们的是，这一地 区正在逐渐发生变化。 第一个建造伟大城邦的人，如乌鲁 克、基什、乌玛、拉加什和乌尔的 国王都是苏美尔人；他们说的是苏 美尔语。 但在公元前 3000 年前后，有新的 一群人搬到了这个地区，和苏美尔 人一起生活。

paragraphs.en: These people spoke theAkkadian language, which is very different from Sumerians. Akkadian is a semitic language, which means that it is part of the same language family as modern Hebrew andArabic. Sumerian is not Semitic, its from a different language family altogether. During the Early Dynastic period, more and moreAkkadian speakers moved into Mespotamia, and over several centuries the people of Sumer gradually became bilingual, speaking both Sumerian and Akkadian. Gradually theAkkadian speakers increased in numbers and strength, and after a while several of the Mesopotamian cities were ruled by
paragraphs.zh: 这些人讲阿卡德语，和苏美尔语很 不一样。 阿卡德语是一种闪族语言，这意味 着它属于与现代希伯来语和阿拉 伯语相同的语系。 苏美尔人不是闪族人，他们的语言 体系完全不一样。 在早王朝时期，越来越多的阿卡德 人搬到了美索不达米亚地区，数百 年来，苏美尔人逐渐学会了同时使 用苏美尔语和阿卡德语。 渐渐地，讲阿卡德语的人越来越 多，权力也越来越大。没过多久， 就有数个美索不达米亚城邦被讲 阿卡德语的国王所统治。

paragraphs.en: Akkadian-speaking Kings, and the majority of the people spoke Akkadian as their first language. In the late 2300s and early 2200s BC, a powerfulAkkadian King emerged in Mesopotamia named Sargon ofAkkad. We don't know very much about Sargon's actual life, because he lived so long ago. The stories about him that we do have were written hundreds of years after he died, so they are part legend and part history. But Sargon ofAkkad was a real person. He founded the first empire in history. For example, one of our main sources for the life of Sargon comes from a Cuneiform tablet called the "Sargon Birth Legend".
paragraphs.zh: 公元前 24 世纪晚期至 23 世纪早 期，一个强大的国王：阿卡德的萨 尔贡王出现在了美索不达米亚地 区。 萨尔贡的历史太过久远，他的真实 生活，我们并不十分了解。关于他 的故事，都是在他死后数百年后写 成的，所以这些故事中有部分是传 奇，部分是史实。 但是阿卡德的萨尔贡是一个真实 存在过的人。他建立了历史上第一 个帝国。 例如，萨尔贡生平的主要资料来源 之一是一块名为“萨尔贡诞生传 奇”的写满楔形文字的石碑。

paragraphs.en: This tablet was written around 800 BC, but Sargon lived around 2300 BC. So our main information about him was written 1500 years after he died. But it’s still a good story, so here is what it has to say: Sargon the mighty King, King of Akkad am I. My mother was lowly, my father I did not know. My city isAzu-Piranu, on the banks of the Euphrates. My lowly mother conceived me in secret, placed me in a basket of reeds coated in bitumen, and cast me down the river, which did not drown me. The river brought me toAki, the irrigator. Who in the goodness of his heart, lifted me out and raised
paragraphs.zh: 这块石碑约创造于公元前 800 年， 而萨尔贡生活在公元前 2300 年前 后。因此，关于萨尔贡生平的主要 资料是在他死后 1500 年前后写成 的。 但这仍是一个不错的故事，它是这 么说的： 我，萨尔贡，乃是阿卡德最强大的 王者。 我的母亲出身卑微，我的父亲无迹 可寻。 我出生在幼发拉底河畔的阿苏皮 拉努，我卑微的母亲秘密地孕育了 我，将我置于一筐涂满沥青的芦苇 中，将我流入河中。河水没有将我 吞没。 我随波逐流，遇到了灌溉者阿齐。 他心地善良，把我养大，把我当作 自己的儿子，任命我为园丁。

paragraphs.en: me as his own son, appointing me gardener. As a gardener the goddess Ishtar loved me and after 4 years I ruled the Kingdom. The black-headed people (Sumerians), I ruled, I governed. After Sargon became King of Kish he went on to conquerAkkad.And then Sargon did something that had never been done before. He created the world's first professional army. In every other army the soldiers were made up of men who had other jobs and could only fight for a limited time. But Sargon's soldiers were professionals. Their main job was fighting. So they could keep getting better and better at it.
paragraphs.zh: 伊师塔女神爱上了我，4 年后，我 统治了这个王国。 我统治着黑发的人民（苏美尔人）， 我统治他们，管理他们。 萨尔贡成为基什国王后，他又征服 了阿卡德。随后，他达成了一项过 去从未达到过的成就。他创建了历 史上第一支职业军队。 其它军队中，战士都是兼职。他们 只在有限的时间内战斗。 但萨尔贡的军队是正规军。战斗是 他们的主要工作。因此他们可以不 断保持进步。

paragraphs.en: The experienced soldiers could pass on their knowledge to new recruits, and a competent corp of officers could develop. Pretty soon, Sargon's army was the most powerful fighting force in the world. In addition to their innovative organization, theAkkadian had another advantage： a new weapon called the compound bow. The oldest style of bow is called a self-bow. This is a bow made from one piece of wood. But a compound bow is more advanced. This is a bow made from two different materials glued together. Compound bows are smaller and more powerful than self bows,
paragraphs.zh: 老兵可以把经验传授给新兵，还可 以从中提拔能力出众的军官。 没过多久，萨尔贡的军队就成为了 最强大的战斗力量。 除了革新的组织结构外，这支军队 还有一项重大优势：一种名为复合 弓的新式武器。 最古老的弓箭是单弓，是一种用整 块木头做成的弓。 但是复合弓更先进。是用两种不同 材料粘在一起做成的弓。 复合弓比单弓更小，也更有力。也 就是说，阿卡德的战士的箭可以比

paragraphs.en: which meant that theAkkadians could shoot farther than their enemies. With his professional army and their compound bows, Sargon was unstoppable. After winning control of Kish and building his army, he moved south and invaded King Zagessi's kingdom. Remember, King Zagessi was the man who had overthrown Urukagina. Sargon defeated Zagessi in battle and captured his capital city of Umma. Then he humiliated Zagessi by putting him in chains and forcing him to wear a dog collar. So Urukagina's curse came true, and Zagessi was forced to wear his sin around his neck.
paragraphs.zh: 他们的敌人射得更远。 有了正规军和复合弓，萨尔贡所向 披靡。 在控制了基什并建立了军队之后， 他向南进发，入侵了扎格西的王 国。 别忘了，是扎格西推翻了乌鲁卡基 那。 萨尔贡在战斗中击败了扎加西，夺 取了他的都城乌玛。他强迫扎格戴 上枷锁和狗项圈，借此羞辱他。于 是乌鲁卡基纳的诅咒成真了，扎格 西将他的罪恶戴在了脖子上。

paragraphs.en: Then Sargon went on to conquer every other Kingdom in Sumer. And when he was done he washed his sword in the Persian Gulf, symbolically showing that nobody stood between his lands and the ocean. He defeated the Elamites and conquered Susa, and then he went up the river and conquered northern Mesopotamia. According to the records Sargon conquered 34 cities, and ruled all the land from the "upper sea, to the lower sea" which might mean all the land between the Mediterranean and the Persian gulf. Here is a map of Sargon's empire: theAkkadian Empire. This is the first empire in history, and it lasted for almost 200 years.
paragraphs.zh: 随后萨尔贡继续征服了苏美尔的 其它王国。当他攻打下所有土地之 后，在波斯湾清洗了他的剑，象征 着没有任何人能够阻挡在他的土 地和海洋之间。 他打败了埃兰，征服了苏萨，随后 沿河而上，统治了整个美索不达米 亚北部。 据记录显示，萨尔贡共征服了 34 个城邦，并且统治了“从上海到下 海”中间的所有土地，这可能意味 着地中海和波斯湾之间的所有陆 地。 这是萨尔贡帝国的地图：阿卡德帝 国。 这是历史上的第一个帝国，它持续 了将近 200 年。

paragraphs.en: This is also the moment in Mesopotamian history when the Akkadian language became dominant over the Sumerian language. Sargon made the city ofAkkad his capital city. I wonder what he must have thought as he sat in his capital looking back upon the accomplishments of his life. He had risen from an abandoned orphan to the most powerful man in the world, the first emperor in human history. Sargon ruledAkkad for 56 years, and died in 2279 BC. Would his children be strong enough to rule the empire after his death, or would everything he built fall apart?
paragraphs.zh: 这也是美索不达米亚历史上，阿卡 德语在苏美尔语言中占主导地位 的时期。 萨尔贡将阿卡德定为都城。 当他坐在都城，回顾一生的成就 时，我很好奇他会想些什么。 他从一个被遗弃的孤儿成长为世 界上最有权力的人，是人类历史上 第一位帝王。 萨尔贡统治阿卡德 56 年，死于公 元前 279 年。他的孩子们会在他死 后继续统治这个帝国，还是他所建 立的一切都将分崩离析？

paragraphs.en: Please join me next time as I tell you the next chapter in this story. Thank you very much. Thank you for listening to this lecture. Now I hope you are ready to learn more about this time period. The three major artifacts mentioned in this story can be found in different museums around the world. The Bronze Head of Sargon can be found at the National Museum of Iraq in Baghdad. We're not 100% that the statue IS Sargon, it may instead be a statue of his son. The cuneiform fragment containing the birth legend of Sargon can be found in the British Museum in London.
paragraphs.zh: 下节课请继续和我一起翻开这个 故事的下一个篇章。 谢谢大家。 感谢大家听完了这一讲，希望你们 还想了解更多关于这段时期的历 史。 在这个故事中提到的三件主要文 物可以在世界各地不同的博物馆 中找到。 在巴格达的伊拉克国家博物馆，你 可以找到萨尔贡的青铜头像。我们 也不能百分之百地确认这座雕像 一定是萨尔贡的，它也可能是他儿 子的雕像。 包含萨贡诞生传说的楔形石碑碎 片可以在伦敦的大英博物馆找到。

paragraphs.en: And the story of Urukagina comes mostly from cuneiform written on a clay cone. This "Cone of Urukagina" can be found in the Louvre in Paris. I would also like to recommend another Youtube channel, if you are able to view them. This one is called Primitive Technology, and in it, the presenter makes many different items from nature, including a bow and arrow. I love using these videos to help my students get a better understanding of the Neolithic period in history. Finally, I would like to once more recommend The Podcast History of Our World by Rob Monaco and The Cartoon History of the Universe by Larry Gonick. Both of these are excellent
paragraphs.zh: 而乌鲁卡基纳的故事则主要记录 在一块圆锥形的黏土块上。这块 “乌鲁卡基纳锥”目前存放于巴黎 卢浮宫。 我还想向你们推荐一个油管频道， 名为《原始技术》。节目中，主持 人用来自自然的材料制作了许多 不同的物品，包括弓和箭。我喜欢 用这些视频来帮助我的学生更好 地理解新石器时代的历史。 最后，我想再次向大家推荐罗伯·摩 纳哥的《世界历史博客》和拉里·戈 尼克的《宇宙漫画史》。 这两个渠道中都有关于萨尔贡和

paragraphs.en: resources and have good information about Sargon and Urukagina. Okay that's it for now, thank you very much!
paragraphs.zh: 乌鲁卡基纳的优质信息。 好的，这节课就到这里，谢谢大家！

titleZh: 第六讲：野蛮人入侵与苏美尔文明的毁灭

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. The written history of humanity is 5000 years old. In those 5 millennia, empires have risen and fallen again and again. Billions of people have been born, lived out their lives, and died. But our essential human nature has not changed. On the time-scale of evolution, 5000 years is no time at all. Our minds are exactly the same now as they were then. As a result, a good student of history will notice that there are patterns that repeat over and over again. One of these patterns is that the founder of a dynasty (like Sargon)
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。 人类有记载的历史有五千年。在这 五千年间，帝国兴衰，朝代更替， 人类不断出生，又走向死亡。 但人性的本质没有变化。在人类进 化的漫长过程中，5000 年不值一 提。当代人的思想，与 5000 年前 相比，并没有本质变化。 因此，一个优秀的历史学生会注意 到，有些模式会不断重复。 其中一种模式是：一个王朝的创始 人（如萨尔贡）必然是一个具有非

paragraphs.en: will be a person of exceptional ability and skill. But his children will not be as strong or capable, and as a result, they will lose power. It may happen in one generation, or it may happen after many, but no dynasty lasts forever. Before I go any further though, I would like to tell you that today's lesson will cover the years between 2334 and 2004 BC. So we are talking about roughly 10 generations over 330 years. Here's what that looks like on our timeline. Anyway, like all dynasties, Sargon's did not last forever. After Sargon died, his son Remush inherited his empire. But once the
paragraphs.zh: 凡才能的人。 打江山易，守江山难。权力交替可 能只要一代人，或需要好几代，但 没有任何一个王朝可以永存。 在我继续讲下去之前，我想告诉你 的是，今天的内容涵盖了公元前 2334-2004年的内容，历时330年， 10 代人。 它在时间线上的位置是这样的。 与其它王朝一样，萨尔贡的帝国也 没能千秋万代。 萨尔贡死后，他的儿子里姆什继承 了他的帝国。老国王刚去世，被征

paragraphs.en: old Emperor was dead, the conquered people saw an opportunity and rebelled. Remush spent the first 9 years of his reign putting down rebellion after rebellion. But then he was murdered by his own men, maybe even by his own brother. This brother, Manesh-Tusu, succeeded him as emperor. But Manesh also had to spend his time quashing revolts. He ruled for 15 years until he was assassinated around 2255 BC. Then his son Naram Sim became the emperor and ruled for 37 years. He spent that time fighting wars and putting down rebellions. And then when he died he was followed by his son Shar-KalAli who ruled until 2193BC. He also
paragraphs.zh: 服的人民看到了机会，就开始反 抗。 里姆什统治的前 9 年间，他始终在 不停地镇压叛乱。但后来，他被自 己人谋杀了，凶手甚至有可能是他 的亲兄弟。 他的这个兄弟，玛尼什吐苏，继承 了他的王位。但玛尼什也不得不把 时间花在镇压叛乱上。他在位 15 年，直到公元前 255 年被暗杀。 后来，他的儿子纳拉姆辛成为了皇 帝，在位 37 年。期间不断打仗和 镇压叛乱。 纳拉姆辛死后，他的儿子沙尔卡利 沙利继承大位，一直到公元前 2193 年，努力维持帝国。

paragraphs.en: struggled to maintain the empire. You see while all of this was happening, the climate was changing. There was a terrible drought around this time, and Mesopotamian farmers had a harder time growing food. But while things were bad in Mesopotamia, they still had the Tigris and Euphrates rivers for a water source. Up in the Zagros mountains, the decrease in rain caused a drought that was much more severe.And so a fierce Nomadic people called the Gutians moved into the river valleys looking for food. The Gutians were wild, nomadic people who had no respect for farming or settled life. This is what theAkkadians had to
paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，气候正在发生变化。在 这段时间里，发生了一场可怕的旱 灾，美索不达米亚的农民在种植粮 食的过程中遇到了困难。 虽然美索不达米亚地区出现了干 旱，但他们还有底格里斯河和幼发 拉底河来提供水源。在扎格罗斯山 脉，降雨量的减少导致了更严重的 干旱。一个名为“古蒂人”的凶猛 的游牧民族进入了河谷地区来寻 找食物。 古蒂人是野蛮的游牧民族，他们不 尊重农业和定居生活。 这就是阿卡德人对古蒂人入侵的

paragraphs.en: say about this Gutian invasion: "They covered the Earth like locusts The great fields produced no grain The fisheries produced no fish And the gardens produced neither honey nor wine." Sargon's descendants fought the Gutian invasion as long as the could, but their land was weakened by drought and eventually they were overwhelmed. Shur-Durul, the lastAkkadian Emperor, was killed by the Gutians in 2154 BC. His capital city ofAkkad was destroyed so completely, that we do not even know where it is. TheAkkadian empire fell after 180
paragraphs.zh: 看法： “他们如同蝗虫过境 田间无粮 塘中无鱼 园中既无蜂蜜，也无美酒。” 萨尔贡的子孙们拼尽全力抵抗古 蒂人，但土地被干旱摧垮，最终他 们也被拖垮。 公元前 2154 年，最后一位阿卡德 国王，沙尔杜尔被古蒂人所杀。 阿卡德都城完全被毁，我们至今无 法确认其位置。 阿卡德王朝在 180 年后垮台，古蒂

paragraphs.en: years, and the Gutians became the dominant force in Mesopotamia. At this time Mesopotamia entered a dark age. There was no central authority, and there is a gap in the written record. This dark age ended after a generation with the resurgence of Sumerian culture. The first Sumerian city to recover from the fall of theAkkadian empire and the Gutian invasion was Lagash. The city where Urukagina lived. In the city of Lagash, a great Sumerian king named Gudea rose in power. By all account he was a wise and skillful ruler. This is what the Sumerians wrote about Gudea:
paragraphs.zh: 人成为了美索不达米亚地区的统 治力量。 这段时间，美索不达米亚地区进入 了黑暗时代。没有中央权威，文字 记录也出现了断代。 一代人的时间过去了，随着苏美尔 文明的复兴，黑暗时代逐渐终结 了。 第一个从阿卡德帝国统治和古蒂 入侵中恢复过来的城邦是拉加什， 即乌鲁卡基纳曾生活的地方。 在拉加什，一个名为古地亚的苏美 尔王开始掌权。他是一个充满智慧 的统治者。 关于古地亚，苏美尔人是这么描述 的：

paragraphs.en: He had everything function as it should in his city Gudea had built the Eninnu, made its powers perfect He had debts remitted and made all hands clear He paid attention to the justice of Nanshi and Ningirsu He provided protection for the orphan against the rich And protection for the widow against the powerful. He had the daughter become the heir in families without a son. Aday of justice dawned for him He set his foot on the neck of evil ones. Gudea was a wise and just King,
paragraphs.zh: 在他的统治下，一切井然有序 古地亚修建了埃尼努神庙，巩固权 力 他清除了债务 他关注南市与宁吉尔苏的正义 他保护孤儿免受富人的侵犯 还保护寡妇免受强权的侵犯 他让女儿成为无子家庭的合法继 承人 对他而言，正义的一天到来了 他将邪恶力量踩在脚下 古地亚是一位睿智公正的帝王，同

paragraphs.en: but also a skilled general. This rare combination of wisdom, justice and military ability allowed Ur to throw off Gutian control and establish an independent Sumerian Kingdom. He ruled Lagash for twenty years from 2144BC to 2124BC. After Gudea died his 19-year old son Ur-Ningirsu followed him onto the throne. Ningirsu was also a good king, but his reign was short. He died in 2119 BC, after only 6 years on the throne. We have found many statues of King Gudea, but we are not as sure what his son looked like. Some people speculate that this statue of a Sumerian man with a shaved head might be King Ur-Ningirsu, so I have included it
paragraphs.zh: 时也是一个经验丰富的将领。这种 智慧、正义和军事能力的罕见结 合，使乌尔摆脱了古蒂的控制，建 立了一个独立的苏美尔王国。从公 元前 2144-2124 年，他统治了拉加 什 20 年。 古地亚死后，他 19 岁的儿子乌尔 宁吉尔苏继承了他的王位。 宁吉尔苏也是一个好国王，但他的 统治很短暂。他死于公元前 2119 年，在位仅 6 年。 我们找到了很多古地亚王的雕像， 但却不清楚他儿子的长相。 有人猜测这个光头的苏美尔人雕 像可能是乌尔宁吉尔苏，所以我把 图片放在这里，好让你在想到这个 名字时，脑海中能出现他的模样。

paragraphs.en: to give you a face and image to go with his name. After Ur-Ningirsu died, Lagash never again was blessed with such good ruler. But at this time, the other Sumerian city states were beginning to rise up again. The next Sumerian city to experience a resurgence was Uruk. Uruk was led by a strong king named Utu-Hengal, and it seems that he came to power in 2119BC, the same year that Ur-Ningirsu died. Utu-Hengal was the king who finally drove the Gutians out of Mesopotamia. He also began to conquer the other Sumerian cities and after he conquered the city of Ur, Utu-Hengal appointed a man
paragraphs.zh: 乌尔宁吉尔苏死后，拉加什再也没 有出现过这么好的统治者。就是在 这段时间，其它苏美尔城邦开始崛 起。 下一个复兴的苏美尔城邦是乌鲁 克。乌鲁克由一个强大的国王领 导，他的名字叫做乌图赫加尔。他 从公元前 2119 年开始掌权，正是 乌尔宁吉尔苏离世的那一年。 乌图赫加尔最终将古蒂人赶出了 美索不达米亚平原。 他还开始攻打其它苏美尔城邦，在 征服了乌尔后，指派乌尔纳姆作为 统治者。

paragraphs.en: named Ur-Namma to be its governor. Ur-Namma was Utu-Hengal's son in law and one of his generals, so this choice made sense. Utu-Hengal ruled for 7 years, but then in 2112 BC he drowned while supervising the construction of a dam. After Utu-Hengal drowned, Ur-Namma—his son-in-law and governor of Ur—seized power. Ur-Namma went on to capture Uruk and then all of the remaining city states. Now for the first time in hundreds of years, Mesopotamia was unified under a Sumerian king. Ur-Namma founded the 3rd Dynasty of Ur, and launched a Sumerian Renaissance, centered
paragraphs.zh: 乌尔纳姆是乌图赫加尔的女婿，也 是他的将军之一，所以这个选择是 说得通的。 乌图赫加尔在位 7 年，但在公元前 2112 年，他在监督修建大坝的过 程中溺水身亡。 乌图赫加尔死后，乌尔纳姆夺取了 政权。 乌尔纳姆接连征服了乌鲁克和其 它苏美尔城邦。这是数百年来第一 次，美索不达米亚平原在一个苏美 尔王的统治下走向了统一。 乌尔纳姆建立了乌尔的第三王朝， 并以他的首都为中心，发起了苏美 尔文化的复兴。

paragraphs.en: around his capital city. Ur-Nammu built the Great Ziggurat of Ur.And when he died in 2094 BC, he was succeeded by his son Shulgi. Shulgi ruled Sumeria for 48 years, and during his reign the 3rd Dynasty of Ur had its golden age. Shugli was a great King. He standardized weights and measures and reformed the calendar. He repaired the roads and infrastructure, and he promoted his officials based on merit. He also finished construction of the Great Ziggurat of Ur, which is probably the most famous building in Sumerian history, and is mentioned by almost all the history textbooks that I read as a kid.
paragraphs.zh: 乌尔纳姆建造了乌尔齐格 拉特金字神塔，当他于公元 前 2094 年去世时，他的儿 子舒尔吉继承了他的职位。 舒尔吉统治了苏美尔统治了 48 年，在他统治期间，乌尔第三王朝 迎来了它的黄金时代。 舒尔吉是一位伟大的国王。他统一 了度与衡，并对历法进行了改革。 他修复了道路和基础设施，并根据 功绩提拔官员。 他还完成了乌尔塔（即齐格拉特神 塔）的修建，这几乎是苏美尔历史 上最有名的建筑物，我小时候读过 的几乎所有历史书上都提到过。

paragraphs.en: Here is an illustration of Ur during its golden age under Ur-Namma and Shulgi. The picture is centered upon the Great Ziggurat. At this point it may even have been the largest city in the world with a population of 65,000 people. The Ziggurat was actually rebuilt in 1985 by Saddam Hussein, who was then the dictator of Iraq. Of course by rebuilding the ziggurat he destroyed a lot of the original structure. Anyway if you see pictures of the Ziggurat that's why it looks so new. The original building was 4000 years old, but the rebuilt version is the same age as me. But perhaps the most important thing that Shugli did was reform
paragraphs.zh: 这是乌尔在乌尔纳姆和舒尔吉的 统治下黄金时代的示意图。这张图 以齐格拉特神塔为中心。 它有可能是当时世界上最大的城 邦，拥有 65,000 人口。 1985 年，萨达姆侯赛因，当时伊 拉克的独裁者，重建了齐格拉特神 塔。 重建神塔也毁坏了一些原始结构。 总之，这就是为什么你能看到这么 新的齐格拉特神塔。虽然最初建于 4000 年前，但翻新后，它的年龄 和我一样。 但舒尔吉最重要的贡献，是他改革 了法律。为了纪念父亲，他编纂了

paragraphs.en: the law code. He called in the "Code of Ur-Namma" in honor of his father, but historians are very confident that Shulgi was the one who created these laws. Shulgi boasted about the justice of his laws saying: I did not deliver the orphan to the rich man The widow to the strong The man with one shekel to the man with one mina Indeed, his system was based on a set of standardized fines and compensation. But for his law code to make sense you need to be familiar with two Sumerian measurements:Ashekel and a mina.
paragraphs.zh: 《乌尔纳姆法典》。历史学家们相 信这些法律是由舒尔吉编写的。 舒尔吉对他法律条文的公正性引 以为傲： 我没有将孤儿交给富人 将寡妇交给权贵 将穷人交给土豪 的确，他的法律体系立根于一系列 标准化的惩罚与赔偿。 想要看懂他的法律条文，你首先需 要熟悉两个苏美尔计量单位：谢克 尔和迈纳。

paragraphs.en: You see the Sumerians did not yet have coins or a formal money system. Instead they used different amounts of silver as their currency. So all of the fines in Shugli's code are given as a certain number of shekels or minas. Ashekel is 11 grams. To help you get a sense of that, I weighed a bunch of coins that I had in my house on my kitchen scale. So as you can see from the picture on the left, a shekel weighs about as much as 4 Yi-mao coins. Amina is 660 grams, that weighs about as much as 200 coins, as you can see from the picture on the right. Now let's take a look at some of the laws in Shulgi's code:
paragraphs.zh: 苏美尔人还没有硬币或正式的货 币体系，他们使用白银来作为流通 货币。因此，《乌尔纳姆法典》中 的罚款都由一定数量的谢克尔或 迈纳来缴纳。 一谢克尔是 11 克，为了辅助你的 理解，我在厨房秤上称了几个硬 币。 如左图所示，一谢克尔约等于 4 个一毛硬币。 一迈纳是 660 克，重量大约相当于 200 个硬币，如右图所示。 现在让我们来看看《乌尔纳姆法 典》中的部分条款：

paragraphs.en: If a man divorces his first wife, she gets one mina of silver. If a man is accused of witch-craft and is found innocent by ordeal The accuser must pay 3 shekels. If a man knocks out the tooth of another man, he shall pay 2 shekels. If a man severs the nose of another man, he must pay 2/3rds of a mina. If a man severs another man's foot, he is to pay 10 shekels. As you can see, Shulgi's law code was based on paying compensation for injuries. The death penalty was only reserved for murder, robbery and rape. Unfortunately, after Shulgi died in 2046 BC, the 3rd Dynasty of Ur went into 4 decades of decline.
paragraphs.zh: 如果一个男人和他的第一任妻子 离婚，她就会得到一迈纳银子。 如果一个人被指控犯有巫术罪，并 在酷刑后被判无罪，原告须支付 3 谢克尔。 如果一个人把另一个人的牙齿敲 掉，他要付 2 谢克尔。 如果一个人割掉了另一个人的鼻 子，他要付 2/3 迈纳。 如果一个人割掉了另一个人的脚， 他要付 10 谢克尔。 正如你所看到的，《乌尔纳姆法典》 是基于对伤害的赔偿。死刑只适用 于谋杀、抢劫和强奸。 不幸的是，公元前 2046 年舒尔吉 死后，乌尔第三王朝进入了长达 40 年的衰落。

paragraphs.en: At this time another group of barbarians called the Martu began moving into Mesopotamia, also because of drought. The Martu also spoke a Semitic language called Amorite, and you will also see them called by that name in the history books. The Sumerian's looked down upon the Martu as Barbarians, but they were also afraid of them. This is what the Sumerians wrote about the Martu: The Martu, who knows no grain, who knows no house nor town Savages who live in the mountans and ignore the gods The Martu. who digs up truffles, who does not bend the knee, and eats raw flesh.
paragraphs.zh: 就在这时，另一群名为“马尔图” 的野蛮人也因为干旱来到了美索 不达米亚。 马尔图人也讲一种闪族语言，名为 亚摩利语。历史书中也将他们成为 亚摩利人。 苏美尔人把马尔图视为野蛮人，但 也害怕他们。关于马尔图人，苏美 尔人是这么描述的： 马尔图人，五谷不分，不识房屋， 不明城镇 住在山上的野蛮人，不敬神祇 马尔图人，茹毛饮血，不弯膝盖

paragraphs.en: He has no house during his life. And when he dies, he will not be buried. For the next 30 years, the Martu continued to push into Sumer and the rest of Mesopotamia. The Kings of Ur tried vainly to keep them out, and even tried building a great wall to protect lower Mesopotamia from these invaders. But it did not work. As the Martu invasions weakened the Sumerians, the Elamites rebelled and drove the Sumerians out of Susa. And then in 2004 BC the Elamites invaded Ur, defeated its army, and burned the city to the ground! Ur was completely destroyed, and the 3rd Dynasty fell along with it.
paragraphs.zh: 生前没有一瓦遮顶，身后无人掩埋 接下来的 30 年间，马尔图人继续 进入苏美尔，以及美索不达米亚的 其他地区。 乌尔国王徒劳地试图阻止他们，甚 至尝试建长城来阻挡这些侵略者， 但都没有用。 马尔图人的入侵削弱了苏美尔人 的力量，这时埃兰反叛了。他们把 苏美尔人赶出了苏萨。 随后在公元前 2004 年，埃兰人入 侵了乌尔，打败了它的军队，把城 邦夷为平地！ 乌尔被彻底摧毁了，第三王朝也随 之灭亡。

paragraphs.en: The destruction of Ur also marks the end of Sumerian civilization and culture. The Sumerian language would never again be the dominant language of Mesopotamia. The Kingdoms that followed would all speakAkkadian or other Semitic languages.And Sumerian would disappear as a language spoken in every-day life, instead it would be used only in religious rituals. One of the last works of Sumerian literature is a lamentation for the destruction of Ur. Now let me end this lesson by reading part of it for you. Oh brick-built Ur, the lament is bitter, the lament made for you Oh city, your name exists, but you have been destroyed
paragraphs.zh: 乌尔的毁灭也标志着苏美尔文明 与文化的终结。苏美尔人的语言不 再是美索不达米亚的主要语言。 随之而来的王朝都将使用阿卡德 语或其它闪族语言。苏美尔语将从 日常生活中消失，成为一种宗教用 语。 苏美尔末期文学里有一篇就是为 乌尔毁灭所作的哀歌。现在我将为 你朗读一段片段。 啊，我的乌尔，苦痛的哀歌为你而 作 啊，我的城市，你的名字永存，但 你已被摧毁

paragraphs.en: Oh city, you wall rises high, but your land has perished Oh city, like an innocent ewe, your lamb has been torn from you Oh Ur, like an innocent goat, your kid has perished ... That city reduced to ruined mounds Breaches had been made in its walls. The people groaned. On its boulevards where festivals had been held, heads lay scattered. In all its street where walks had been taken, corpses were piled, they made the blood of the land flow. Its corpses like fat left in the sun, melted away of themselves Men struck down with the spear were not bound with bandages As if in the place where their
paragraphs.zh: 啊，我的城市，你高墙永驻，但你 的土地尽毁 啊，我的城市，你就像一只无辜的 母羊，被生生夺走了孩子 啊，我的乌尔，你就想一只无辜的 山羊，你的孩子已被摧毁 乌尔已经毁灭，哀歌伴着痛苦。 鲜血充满洞穴，如铜水充满模铸。 尸体溶解，如烈日下的脂肪； 神庙崩垮，城市被烟云遮挡。 血流如河淌，男女把哀歌唱。 萦绕着悲伤，乌尔已不常。

paragraphs.en: mother had labored, they lay in their own blood. The weak and the strong of Ur perished from hunger. Mothers and Fathers who did not leave their houses were consumed by fire.
