The little ones lying in their mother's arms were carried off like fish by the waters. ... Oh Nana! (the god of Sumer) The humble people who lay hold of your feet have brought you their tears. The smitten house playing music before you. May the black-headed people cast away from you, make obeisance to you. In your city reduced to ruined
paragraphs.zh: 父神南纳啊，市镇只剩废墟。万众 哀哭。 在那日风暴离了城市：万众哀哭。 那是尸骨，不是陶片，遗弃在入口 上。 城墙土崩瓦解，高耸的城门道，堆 满了死亡者。 宽阔的大街上，万众曾聚会宴乐， 而今却躺在杂乱中。 广袤的田野里，人们曾载歌载舞，

paragraphs.en: mounds, may a lament be made to you. Until distant days, other days, future days. So with that we shall end this lesson. I hope to see you in future days as we look at the next great Mesopotamian civilization. Thank you very much I hope you enjoyed that lesson and are eager to learn more. Many of the artifacts from this lecture, like the King Gudea statue, the Man with the shaved head, or the lament for Ur can all be found in the Louvre museum. Shulgi's laws, the Code of Ur-Namma can be found in the IstanbulArchaeology Museum in Istanbul Turkey.
paragraphs.zh: 而今却躺在尸堆上。 这节课到这里就结束了。希望接下 来几天还能再和大家一起学习下 一节课：下一个出现在美索不达米 亚的灿烂文明。 谢谢大家。希望你们喜欢这节课， 并且还想学习更多。 这节课中提到的许多工艺品，例如 古地亚雕像，光头男性雕像，以及 《乌尔哀歌》，都收藏在卢浮宫。 舒尔吉编纂的《乌尔纳姆法典》目 前收藏在土耳其伊斯坦布尔的伊 斯坦布尔考古博物馆。

paragraphs.en: Ur and its Ziggurat can be found in souther Iraq, near Tell el-Muqayyar in the Dhi Qar Governate. Finally, two great websites are listed here. The one for the Louvre is really good of course. The one called "Sumerian Shakespeare" is not as academic or rigorous, it seems to be the work of a history enthusiast rather than a proper historian, but its fun to read and has a lot of information, even if the author does mix in his own speculation a little too much. All right, everyone. That concludes today’s lecture. I’ll see you next time.
paragraphs.zh: 乌尔和齐格拉特神塔目前 位于伊拉克南部，靠近济加 尔省的穆喀雅废丘。 最后，这里列出了两个非常好的网 站。卢浮宫的官网自然是极好的资 源。 这个名为“苏美尔莎士比亚”的网 站，并不是一个严格意义上的学术 网站，它更像是一个历史爱好者的 作品，而不是真正的历史学家的著 作。尽管作者加入了自己的猜想， 但读起来依然非常有趣，并且信息 量满满。 今天的课程就进行到这里，我们下 节课再见。

titleZh: 第七讲：巴比伦的崛起与一代雄主汉谟拉比

paragraphs.en: In the last lessons we learned about how theAmorites invaded Mesopotamia and then weakened the 3rd Dynasty of Ur, the last Sumerian Kingdom. Then the Elamites attacked the weakened Sumerians from the east and destroyed the capital city of Ur in 2006 BC. After the destruction of Ur, Mesopotamia entered another dark age as the Semitic-speakingAmorites became the dominant ethnic group in the region. But here is another one of those historical patterns. When a nomadic barbarian people conquers a settled urban culture, the nomads eventually adopt the culture of the city dwellers
paragraphs.zh: 上节课我们学习的内容有：亚摩利 人入侵了美索不达米亚，削弱了乌 尔第三王朝——最后一个苏美尔 王国的力量。 接下来，埃兰人又从东边给羸弱的 乌尔第三王朝一击，并于公元前 2006 年摧毁了其首都乌尔城。 在乌尔城被毁后，美索不达米亚进 入了又一个黑暗时期，而这一时期 的主宰者是说着闪米特语的亚摩 利人。 这又是一种典型的历史模式：当野 蛮的游牧民族征服定居的城邦民 族之后，他们最终会采取城邦民族 的文化，也变得文明起来。

paragraphs.en: and become civilized. Nomadic peoples are sometimes stronger on the battlefield than urban people. But urban people almost always have a stronger culture. This often happens in Chinese history. In the 1200s the Chinese Song dynasty was defeated by the Mongol Yuan dynasty.And in the 1600s the Chinese Ming were defeated by the Manchurian Qing. But even though the Mongols and the Manchurians were stronger on the battlefield, they eventually adopted the Chinese way of life, because the urban, agricultural Chinese had a stronger culture than the nomadic Mongols or Manchurians. The civilization that we will be focusing on today is Old Babylon, which lasted from 1830 to 1530 BC,
paragraphs.zh: 在战场上，游牧民族是强者，但在 文化方面，城邦民族往往更胜一 筹。 这一点在中国历史上尤为常见。13 世纪，宋朝被蒙古人的元朝取而代 之，17 世纪，明朝被满族人的清朝 取而代之。 不过，战场上的胜者蒙古人、满族 人却最终接受了汉族人的生活方 式，这就是因为定居农耕的汉人拥 有更强的文化力量。 我们今天学习的重点是古巴比伦 文明。从时间轴上我们可以看到， 它持续的时间是公元前 1830-1530

paragraphs.en: this is what that looks like on our timeline. The same thing happened in Mesopotamian history. TheAmorite invasions led to a dark age that lasted for 2 centuries. But during that time, they saw the benefits of settled, urban life, and they began to copy Sumerian culture. By the 1800s BC, theAmorites had become civilized. They lived in cities and farmed, and had adopted much of Sumerian culture, including Cuneiform writing. But the language they spoke was Amorite not Sumerian.And since Amorite is a similar language to Akkadian, and everyone already spokeAkkadian, it became the common language of Mesopotamia in the 2nd millennium BC.
paragraphs.zh: 年。 同样的事情也发生在了美索不达 米亚的历史上。 亚摩利人的入侵带来了长达 2 个世 纪的黑暗时期，但在这一时期，亚 摩利人看到了定居、城邦生活的好 处，所以他们走上了复制苏美尔文 化之路。 公元前 1800 年，亚摩利人已经改 头换面，成了文明人。他们住在城 里，农耕为生，还吸纳了许多苏美 尔文化，包括楔形文字。 但是他们依旧说亚摩利语，而非苏 美尔语。同时，由于亚摩利语与阿 卡德语比较相似，且人人都会说阿 卡德语，因此到了公元前 2000 年， 亚摩利语成为了美索不达米亚的 通用语言。

paragraphs.en: At this point, two cities in Mesopotamia became very important: Nineveh and Babylon. Both of these cities were inhabited by people who spoke Semitic languages. The people of Nineveh then founded the city ofAssur.AndAssur became their political capital while Nineveh was their religious capital and ancestral home. These people fromAssur became know as theAssurian orAssyrians. Meanwhile the other great city was called "Babeli" inAkkadian, "Babel" in Hebrew, and "Babylon" in Greek. English historians use the Greek name, so we will call these people "The Babylonians."
paragraphs.zh: 这一时期，美索不达米亚有两座城 邦十分重要：尼尼微和巴比伦。 两座城里的人们都说闪米特语。 尼尼微人民随后建立了亚述城，并 以其为政治首都，尼尼微则成为他 们的宗教首都及故土。 而这些来自亚述城的人就是大名 鼎鼎的亚述人。 同时，另一座大城邦在阿卡德语中 叫做“巴贝利”，在希伯来语中叫 做“巴贝尔”，在希腊语中则叫“巴 比伦”。 英语历史学家沿用了希腊语名称， 所以我们称这些人为“巴比伦人”。

paragraphs.en: For the next 1500 years of Mesopotamian history, the two most powerful nations will be the Babylonians and theAssyrians. So the city ofAshur is the origin of the word "Assyrian", which describes the people who come from that part of the world. And the name "Assyrian" is also the origin of the word "Syria" which is a country in that part of the world. So Iraq is named after the Mesopotamian city of Uruk. And Syria is named after the Mesopotamian city ofAshur. Anyway, Babylon was settled by these civilizedAmorites, and the first Babylonian king was anAmorite named Sumu-Abum. He came to power around 1894 BC. But he was a
paragraphs.zh: 在接下来 1500 年的美索不达米亚 历史中，最强大的民族便是巴比伦 人与亚述人。 所以，亚述人之名来源于亚述城， 表示生活在某地的人。 而“亚述人”也是“叙利亚”国名 的来源。 所以说，伊拉克是以美索不达米亚 乌鲁克城命名的。 而叙利亚则以美索不达米亚的亚 述城命名。 无论如何，文明开化后的亚摩利人 建起了巴比伦，第一任巴比伦国 王，亚摩利人苏姆阿布姆，于公元 前 1894 年掌权，他爱好和平，专 注于国防与农业。

paragraphs.en: peaceful King who focused on defense and agriculture. 60 years later, in 1833 BC another Amorite King named Shamshi-Aadad came to power in a kingdom called Ekallatum, a little to the north of Babylon. Shamshi-Adad conquered the Assyrians and his other neighbors, the Kingdom of Mari and built an empire.And then he put his sons in charge. Shamsi-Aadad also continually threatened Babylon and demanded tribute from them. But when Shamsi-Hadad died in 1776 BC, one of his sons was overthrown, while the other desperately clung to power inAssur. Archaeologists have done
paragraphs.zh: 60 年后，到了公元前 1833 年，另 一位名叫沙姆希-阿达德的亚摩利 国王在巴比伦北边的伊卡莱特姆 城即位。 沙姆希-阿达德征服了亚述人及其 邻居马里王国，建立了一个帝国， 并让他的儿子们掌权。 沙姆希-阿达德也不断地威胁着巴 比伦，要求他们进贡。 但是，公元前 1776 年，当沙姆希- 阿达德去世后，他某个儿子的政权 被推翻，另一个则牢牢掌控着亚述 城。 考古学家在沙姆希-阿达德的王国

paragraphs.en: excavations in the area around Shamshi-Adad's kingdom and they have found inscriptions claiming that Shamshi-Adad built the temple to Enlil at Ashur. Here is a drawing of what that building may have looked like. But even more interestingly, they have also found a price list which tells us how much things cost during Shamshi-Adad's rule. Apparently, 1 shekel of silver could buy 2 kor of barley, 15 minas of wool, or 2 seahs of oil. We talked about shekels and mina's last class, and to put those into modern terms: 11 grams of silver could buy 276 kilograms of barley, 9.9 kilograms of wool, and 14.6 kilograms of oil.
paragraphs.zh: 周围地区进行了挖掘，发现了声称 沙姆希-阿达德在亚述城为恩利尔 建造神庙的铭文。图片中展示的图 纸就可能是神庙曾经的样子。 更有趣的是，他们也找到了一个价 格清单，上面列出了沙姆希-阿达 德统治时期的物品价值。 很显然，1 谢克尔的银可以买 2 固 尔的大麦，15 迈纳的羊毛或 2 细亚 的油。 我们上节课讨论过谢克尔和迈纳， 转换成现代计量单位则是： 11克白银可以买276公斤大麦，9.9 公斤羊毛和 14.6 公斤油。

paragraphs.en: I think price lists like this are very helpful for giving us a "feel" for what daily life was like 3800 years ago. In 1792, while Shamsi-Hadad was still King ofAssyria, anAmorite man named Hammurabi became the King of Babylon. For the first 16 years of his rule, he focused on trying to hold off the Assyrians and also reform the Babylonian economy. When Shamsi-Hadad died in 1776, Hammurabi got a little breathing room. But then the King of Elam, Siwe-Palar-Khuppak, started sending Hammurabi threatening letters. He demanded that the Babylonians give him some of their territory: The Cities of Eshnuna that you hold,
paragraphs.zh: 这样的价格清单可以更好地帮助 我们“感受”3800 年前的日常生活。 公元前 1792 年，亚述的国王依旧 是沙姆希-阿达德，但巴比伦国的 国王却换成了一个名叫汉谟拉比 的亚摩利人。 在汉谟拉比在治的头 16 年中，他 专注于抵御亚述人，并在巴比伦进 行经济改革。 公元前 1776 年，沙姆希-阿达德辞 世，汉谟拉比才有了一丝喘息之 机。 但很快，埃兰国王西维-巴拉尔-库 帕克开始向汉谟拉比发送威胁信 件，要求巴比伦人给他土地： 你拥有的埃示努那城

paragraphs.en: Do they not belong to me? Release them and submit them to my yoke Otherwise I will pillage your country. My army would set out to the city Mankasun And it would cross the river at that spot At the head of my army, I myself would cross the river and invade your country. Then the King of Elam started demanding that Hammurabi send him soldiers for a war against the Kingdom of Larsa. But Hammurabi was suspicious and talked to the King of Larsa, and it turned out that the King of Elam was also asking Larsa for troops to attack
paragraphs.zh: 难道不属于我吗？ 把他们上交给我 否则我会踏平你的国家 我的军队将前往曼卡孙 他们会跨过河流 作为领军人，我也会跨过河流 攻占你的国家 后来，埃兰国王开始要求汉谟拉比 派遣士兵去攻打拉尔萨王国。 但汉谟拉比起了疑心，并告知了拉 尔萨国王，结果发现埃兰国王同样 要求拉尔萨国派兵攻打巴比伦国。

paragraphs.en: Babylon. Angry at this double-cross Hammurabi formed an alliance with the Kingdoms of Mari and Shamshi-Hadad's eldest son in Assyria, and together they fought a war with Elam. The war went on for many years, until the King of Elam asked for peace in 1764. But, Rim-Sim, the King of Larsa never helped Hamurabbi, even though he had also been targeted by Elam. So after the war with Elam, Hamurabbi vowed revenge against this treacherous King. He then attacked Larsa and overthrew Rim-Sim. He then went on to win wars against several other Kings, and by 1759 BC, Hamurabbi had built Babylon into a great
paragraphs.zh: 一怒之下，汉谟拉比联合马里国与 沙姆希-阿达德在亚述的长子，举 兵攻打埃兰王国。 战争持续了许多年，最终以公元前 1764 年埃兰王国求和而告终。 拉尔萨国王瑞姆辛即便也曾被埃 兰国针对过，但他从未帮助过汉谟 拉比。所以在与埃兰国之战结束 后，汉谟拉比发誓要报复这个背信 弃义的国王。 于是他攻打拉尔萨，推翻了瑞姆 辛。接着他又打了几场胜仗，到了 公元前 1759 年，汉谟拉比将巴比 伦建成了一个强大的帝国。

paragraphs.en: Empire. Hamurabbi then decided the time had come to stop fighting wars, and start reorganizing and consolidating his empire. And like Shulgi Hummurabi created a code of laws to govern his empire. Hamurabbi's code of laws is the most famous things about him. But I think his laws were not as good or humane as Shulgi's Code of Ur-Nama.And there is evidence that Hamurabbi's Code was built on Shulgi's example. But, Hamurabbi's code is much more famous, probably because we have a copy written on a beautiful black Stele. And this stele sits in the Louvre museum, in Paris, one of the most famous museums in the world. So all
paragraphs.zh: 接下来，汉谟拉比决定停战，开始 重组整顿他的帝国 跟舒尔吉一样，汉谟拉比制定了一 套法律来管理他的帝国。 汉谟拉比法典最为出名。但我认为 他的法律不如舒吉尔的乌尔纳姆 法典人性化。有证据表明汉谟拉比 法典是建立在舒吉尔的法典之上 的。 但是汉谟拉比法典更出名，可能是 流传了一副漂亮黑石碑的原因吧。 这座石碑现收藏于世界最出名的 博物馆之一——巴黎卢浮宫。因 此，所有的历史书都在讨论汉谟拉

paragraphs.en: of the history textbooks will talk about Hamurabbi's code, but only the really good ones will mention Shulgi! Unlike the code of Ur-Nama, Hamurabbi's code was based not on fines and compensation, but on corporal punishment and physical retaliation. Let's take a look at some of its punishments. If a noble blinds the eye of another noble, they shall blind his eye. If he breaks the bone of another noble, they shall break his bone. If a builder builds a house for a man, and does not make his work strong, and if the house he built collapses and kills the owner, that builder shall be killed.
paragraphs.zh: 比法典，但只有真正优秀的教科书 才会提到舒尔吉！ 不像乌尔纳姆法典，汉谟拉比法典 不是基于罚款和赔偿，而是基于肉 刑和身体报复。 倘一个贵族毁其他贵族之目，则毁 其目 倘一个贵族断其他贵族之骨，则断 其骨 如果一个建筑工搭建的房屋倒塌， 若压死了屋主，把建筑工处死，若 压死的是房主的儿子，就把建筑工 的儿子处死

paragraphs.en: If he kills the owner's son, the son of the builder shall be killed. If a noble blinds the eye of a commoner, or breaks the bone of a commoner, he shall pay 500 grams of silver. If he blinds the eye of a slave, or breaks the bones of slave, he shall pay 1/2 his price. As you can see, the code prescribes hurting the criminal in the same way he or she hurt the victim. But only if the criminal and victim are of the same social class. If someone from a higher social class hurts someone from a lower social class, they will not get the same punishment. But despite its flaws, Hammurabi's law code was intended to serve justice. In fact, this is what Hammurabi thought about his laws:
paragraphs.zh: 如果一个贵族损毁了平民的眼睛， 或打断了平民的骨头 则应赔偿 500 克白银 如果他损毁了奴隶的眼睛，或打断 了奴隶的骨头 则应赔偿奴隶一半的价格 正如你所见，法典规定以受害者受 害的方式惩罚罪犯，但是，只有当 罪犯和受害者属于同一社会阶层 时该规则才成立。 如果较高社会 阶层的人伤害来自较低社会阶层 的人，他们就不必受到同样的惩 罚。 但是除了这些缺点，汉谟拉比法典 旨在维护公平正义。事实上，汉谟 拉比是这样看待他的法典的：

paragraphs.en: Let my name be ever repeated Let the oppressed, who has a case at law Come and stand before this, my image as king of justice Let him read the inscription And understand my precious words The inscription will explain his case to him He will find out what is just And his heart will be glad In future times, through all coming generations Let the King who may be in the land Observe the words of righteousness Hammurabi's laws and policies kept his empire stable for at least 42
paragraphs.zh: 让我的名字永远反复称颂 让有例可循的被压迫的人 来吧，站在这里，站在我正义之王 的形象之前 让他读一读这碑文 理解我宝贵的话语 碑文会解释他的案例 他会发现什么是正义 他会满心欢喜 在将来，在子孙后代 让这片土地的国王 看看这正义的话语 汉谟拉比的法律和政策使他的帝 国至少稳定了 42 年， 但是当他生

paragraphs.en: years. But when he grew ill and died in 1750, the Babylonian Empire began to weaken. The Babylonians began to deplete their topsoil, which led to smaller harvests and a shrinking population. They also faced problems with barbarian raiders. And then, in the 1500s BC a new powerful kingdom began to rise in Anatolia, what is today modern Turkey. These new people were called the "Hatti" or "Hittites" And then in 1531, Hammurabi's last descendant, King Samsu-Ditana was defeated by a man named Mursilis: a King of the Hittite Empire. Then the Hittites sacked the capital city of Babylon, and brought the Old-Babylonian empire to a close.
paragraphs.zh: 病并于 1750 年去世时，巴比伦帝 国开始衰弱。 巴比伦人的表层泥土资源枯竭了， 这进一步导致了收成和人口减少。 他们还遭遇了蛮族入侵。 到了公元前 1500 年，一个新的强 盛帝国在安纳托利亚崛起，也就是 如今的土耳其地区，这就是后来人 们所称的“哈提”或“赫梯人”。 公元前 1531 年，汉谟拉比最后一 位继承人，萨姆苏迪塔纳被赫梯国 王穆尔西里什击败。 然后赫梯攻占了巴比伦首都，终结 了古巴比伦帝国。 赫梯人并没有 留在这里，但在击败巴比伦人之

paragraphs.en: The Hittites did not stay, but after they defeated the Babylonians, power in Mesopotamia shifted north toAssyria. The Hittites were not Mesopotamian, they lived in theAnatolian peninsula, what is today modern Turkey. So now we have reached a point where we can no longer look at the history of Mesopotamia in isolation, the rest of the world is starting to have an effect. This means we will stop our study of Mesopotamian history here, with the fall of the Old-Babylonian Empire in 1531 BC.And we will begin looking at what was happening in the rest of the world.
paragraphs.zh: 后，美索不达米亚的权力向北转移 到了亚述。 赫梯人不是美索不达米亚人，他们 生活在安纳托利亚半岛，也就是如 今的土耳其。 所以现在我们已经不能孤立地区 看待美索不达米亚的历史了，世界 其它地方也开始对其产生影响。 随着公元前 1531 年古巴比伦帝国 的衰落，我们对美索不达米亚历史 的学习就先到这儿，接下来我们会 看一看世界上别的地方发生了什 么故事。

paragraphs.en: We will start that journey around the rest of the BronzeAge world by looking at Egypt, the other great ancient Kingdom, which has a history just as long as Mesopotamia. Thank you very much! I hope you enjoyed that lesson on the Babylonians. If you want to learn more, I have some recommendations for you. The most important artifact which we discussed today was the Code of Hammurabi. It is on display in the Louvre Museum in Paris. Just like so many other ancient artifacts. And I would like to recommend a podcast, which I think is the greatest history podcast today. It is called Hardcore History by Dan Carlin. The most recent episodes are free and the
paragraphs.zh: 我们的青铜时代他处之旅将从埃 及开始，这是一个有着和美索不达 米亚同样悠久历史的古老王国。 谢谢大家！ 我希望你们能喜欢这节关于巴比 伦王国的课程，如果你想了解更 多，我这里有一些推荐。 我们今天讨论的最重要的文物是 汉谟拉比法典。就像许多其他古代 文物一样，它陈列在巴黎的卢浮宫 博物馆。 我还想推荐一个播客，我认为这是 最棒的历史播客，丹·卡林称之为 硬核历史，最新的剧集是免费的， 此前旧的剧集售价 1 或 2 美元。

paragraphs.en: older ones can be bought for 1 or 2 dollars. But each podcast is a gripping 4-5 hour long story about a period in world history. In terms of Middle Eastern history, I would recommend the episodes titled "King of Kings" or "Judgement at Nineveh" Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 每集都长达 4-5 小时，扣人心弦地 讲述某一时期的历史。 就中东历史而言，我推荐“王者中 的王者”或“评价尼尼微”这一集。 谢谢大家！

titleZh: 第八讲：人类文明闪耀时——古埃及

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today, for the first time in this series, we are going to leave Mesopotamia behind and begin looking at the rest of the world.And we will start with Egypt. Today we will be focusing on the origins of Egyptian civilization and the Old Kingdom of Egypt, which is a period of about 1000 years from 3100 to 2100 BC. This is what that looks like on our timeline. My decision to begin our story with Sumer instead of Egypt was largely arbitrary. Egypt and Sumer are the two oldest
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列 课程。 今天，这是本系列的第一次，我 们将离开美索不达米亚，开始关 注世界的其他地方。我们将从埃 及开始。 今天，我们将关注埃及文明的起 源和埃及古王国时期，也就是约 公元前 3100-2100 年，持续 1000 年左右。 这是它在时间轴上的样子。 我决定用苏美尔而不是埃及来开 始我们的故事，这在很大程度上 是武断的。 埃及和苏美尔是地球上最古老的

paragraphs.en: civilizations on Earth.And by that I mean they both developed cities and writing at roughly the same time: 3000 BC. So I could have started this class by talking about Egypt instead of Sumer. They are both about the same age. Both civilizations developed in River valleys. For Egypt it was the Nile River valley. In fact, the Greek Historian Herodotus has called Egypt: "The Gift of the Nile". And if you look at a satellite picture of the Nile you can see why. On either side of the river valley, Egypt is harsh desert. But along the Nile it is wet and green and good for farming.
paragraphs.zh: 两大文明。我的意思是，他们几 乎在同一时间发展了城市和文 字：公元前 3000 年。 因此，这节课我们可以从埃及谈 起。埃及和苏美尔的古老程度是 比较相近的。 这两个文明都是在河谷中发展起 来的。 埃及是从尼罗河发展起来的。事 实上，希腊历史学家希罗多德将 埃及称为“尼罗河的馈赠”。 如果你看一看尼罗河的卫星图 片，你就会明白为什么。在河谷 的任何一边，埃及都是酷热的沙 漠。但是沿着尼罗河两岸的地区 是湿润的，绿色的，对农业是有 益的。

paragraphs.en: Now Egypt is defined by the Nile. Since the Nile flows from South to North, the southern part of the Nile has a higher elevation than the northern part. It is higher up. So for this reason we call southern Egypt as Upper Egypt, and northern Egypt as Lower Egypt. This can be a little confusing, since I know I am used to thinking of North as "up." But you just have to think about the direction that the Nile flows, and it will make sense. Now upper Egypt ends at a place called "The First Cataract". This slide shows you a picture of cataract.Acataract is rocky place where it is impossible to sail a boat. The water is too shallow and rocky
paragraphs.zh: 尼罗河定义了埃及。由于埃及南 部地区海拔高于北部，尼罗河的 流向是从南往北。 因此，我们称埃及南部为上埃及， 埃及北部为下埃及。 这可能有点容易让人混淆，因为 通常我们都遵守“上北下南”的 惯例。 但是你只需要考虑尼罗河的流 向，这就能说得通了。 上埃及在“第一大瀑布”处就终 结了。 这张图片展示了瀑布的样子。瀑 布是一个无法航行通过的地方， 这里水太浅，岩石很多。人们必 须得下船，步行一段距离，然后

paragraphs.en: here. So people had to get out, walk, and then change to another boat. Therefore, these cataracts became a natural barrier for transportation, communication and trade. The Nile river has 6 of these cataracts, and they form a natural boundary to Egyptian civilization. Even today, the 2nd cataract roughly marks the border between Egypt and Sudan. Now the Egyptians developed farming around 5000 BC, and by 3000 BC, they had developed into two separate Kingdoms. The Kingdom of Upper Egypt and the Kingdom of Lower Egypt. The King of Upper Egypt wore a white crown (which to me looks
paragraphs.zh: 再上另一条船。 因此，这些瀑布成为了交通、通 讯和贸易的天然屏障。 尼罗河有 6 道这样的瀑布，它们 形成了埃及文明的自然边界。 即使在今天，第二道瀑布也大致 标志着埃及和苏丹之间的边界。 埃及人在公元前 5000 年左右发展 了农业，到公元前 3000 年，他们 发展成两个独立的王国:上埃及 王国和下埃及王国。 上埃及的国王戴着一顶白色的王 冠（对我来说，它看起来就像一

paragraphs.en: like a bowling pin), this was called the Hedjet. The King of Lower Egypt wore a red crown which was called the Deshret. But around 3000 BC, the King of Upper Egypt, who was named Narmer or Menes unified the two Kingdoms and became the first Pharoah of a united Egypt. To symbolize the unity of the Kingdoms, Narmer wore a crown that was a combination of the Hedjet and Deshret. This combined crown was called the Pschent. Egyptian civilization was able to grow so much food because the Nile River flooded every year. These annual floods would deposit
paragraphs.zh: 个保龄球瓶），它被称为“上埃及 白王冠”。 下埃及的国王戴着一顶红色的王 冠，叫做“红冠”（也称“德施雷 特冠”）。 但大约在公元前 3000 年，上埃及 的国王，名为那美尔或美纳斯， 统一了两个王国，成为了首位统 一埃及的法老。 为了象征王国的统一，那美尔戴 着一顶结合了白王冠和红冠特征 的新王冠。 这顶王冠被称为“双重王冠” 埃及文明能够种植如此多的粮 食，是因为尼罗河每年都会发一 次大水。 每年的洪水都会沉积一层淤泥，

paragraphs.en: a layer of silt, which would replenish the topsoil and keep the farmlands fertile year after year. Here is a picture of the Nile flooding. This rich soil meant that Egyptian agriculture was very productive, and Egypt could grow a large amount of food. This picture should give you some idea of what Egyptian farming was like. But in 1970 the Egyptian government finished building the Aswan Dam. This Dam is one of the largest Dams in the world, and it was able to control the annual floods and produce lots of electricity. Unfortunately, this meant that
paragraphs.zh: 补充表层土壤，并使农田年复一 年地肥沃。 这是一张尼罗河洪水的图片。 肥沃的土壤意味着埃及的农业产 量很高，可以收获大量的粮食。 这张照片应该能让你了解埃及的 农业是什么样的。 但在 1970 年，埃及政府完成了阿 斯旺大坝的建设。这座大坝是世 界上最大的水坝之一，它能够控 制每年的洪水，并产生大量的电 力。 不幸的是，这意味着埃及的表层

paragraphs.en: Egyptian topsoil was no longer replenished by fresh deposits of silt. And so a way of life that had existed for at least 7000 years came to an end. Now the Egyptians have to use chemical fertilizers to grow food, but at least they don't have to deal with floods anymore. These are the sorts of trade-offs that large dam projects entail. China made similar decisions when it built the 3 Gorges dam on the Yangtze River. The ancient Egyptians also paid close attention to the seasons and the stars. And for them one star was particularly important. This is the star Sirius. It is also called the Dog Star.
paragraphs.zh: 土壤不再有新鲜淤泥的补充。因 此，一种已经存在了至少 7000 年 的生活方式就此终结了。 现在埃及人不得不使用化肥来种 植粮食，但至少他们不再需要处 理洪水了。这些都是大型水坝项 目所需要的权衡。 中国在长江上修建三峡大坝时也 做出了类似的决定。 古埃及人也密切关注着季节和繁 星。 对他们来说，有一颗星星尤为重 要。这就是天狼星。它也被称为 “狗之星”。

paragraphs.en: Sirius is actually one of the brightest stars in the sky, and since it is only 10 light years away, it also one of the closest stars to Earth. The Egyptians noticed that every year, Sirus would rise above the Eastern Horizon just before dawn. And they saw that short after Sirius appeared the Nile would begin to flood. So Sirius became a very important Star to them. They believed it had deep mystical connection to the annual floods. Because the flooding of the Nile was so important, the Egyptians built their calendar around it. The Egyptian year had three seasons. June to September was called
paragraphs.zh: 天狼星实际上是天空中最亮的恒 星之一，因为它离地球只有 10 光 年，它也是离地球最近的恒星之 一。 埃及人注意到，每一年，天狼星 都会在黎明前从东方的地平线上 升起。 天狼星出现后不久，尼罗河就会 开始泛滥。 因此，天狼星成为了一颗非常重 要的星星。他们相信这与每年的 洪水有着深切的神秘联系。 由于尼罗河洪水对埃及人来说如 此重要，他们还针对汛期制定了 历法。 埃及每年有 3 季。 6 月至 9 月被称为“阿赫特”，当

paragraphs.en: "Akhet" and it was the time of flood. October to January was the time to plant their fields and grow your crop. This was called "Peret". And February to June was called "Shomu" which means "the harvest". So you can see the entire Egyptian way of life was built around the floods.And during the flood season, none of the country could work on farming. So there was a lot of available labor during those months. And it was around the year 2680 BC, the Pharoah Djoser and his Grand Vizer Imhotep decided to build the very first pyramid. The pyramid was a giant tomb,
paragraphs.zh: 时是洪水泛滥的时候。 10 月到 1 月是种植庄稼的季节， 被称为“冬季”。 2 月至 6 月被称为“夏矛”，意思 是“收获”。 所以你可以看到，整个埃及的生 活方式都是围绕洪水展开的。在 汛期，没有任何国家可以在农田 里耕作。所以在这几个月里有很 多可用的劳动力。 大约在公元前 2680 年，左赛尔法 老和他的维西尔（即宰相）英霍 蒂普决定修建世界上第一座金字 塔。 金字塔是一个巨大的坟墓，用于

paragraphs.en: meant to guard Djoser's body for its journey into the underworld.And it was also designed as a building project to employ out-of-work Egyptians during the flood season. There is a misperception from Hollywood movies that the Pyramids were built by slaves. But this is not true. We have evidence that the pyramid builders competed with each other in teams (because they have proudly written graffiti on the stones that boasts about how much their team built.) Injured workers received medical care like having broken bones set. The workers were fed meat every day, which was a rare luxury for peasants in the Bronze age. But they needed that protein for all the hard work they were doing.
paragraphs.zh: 保护左赛尔的遗体进入地下世 界。它还被设计成一个建筑项目， 在洪水季节为失业的埃及人提供 工作。 好莱坞电影中有一种误解，认为 金字塔是由奴隶建造的。实际并 不是这样。我们有证据表明，金 字塔的建造者在团队中相互竞争 （因为他们自豪地在石头上涂 鸦，吹嘘他们的团队建造了多 少。） 工人如果受了伤（例如骨折），可 以得到医疗救护。 工人们每天都有肉吃，这对青铜 时代的农民来说是一种难得的奢 侈。但是他们需要这种蛋白质来 完成他们的工作。

paragraphs.en: And we know that Pyramid builders did not have to pay taxes for each year that they worked. So the Pyramids were really a jobs program that employed the people during the flood-season and increased the prestige and power of the pharaoh. In fact, we think the Egyptians were tremendously proud of their pyramids. Djoser's pyramid was the first, and the project worked so well, that it began an era of pyramid building which lasted for 300 years. And it wasn't just the Pyramids, the Sphinx was also built at this time, at around 2550 BC. You can go see the Pyramids and Sphinx today. You must remember
paragraphs.zh: 而且我们也知道，金字塔的建造 者并不交税。 因此，金字塔实际上是一个就业 项目，在洪水季节雇佣了劳动力， 增加了法老的威望和权力。 事实上，我们认为埃及人对他们 的金字塔感到非常自豪。 左赛尔的金字塔是第一个，这个 项目运作得很好，它开始了一个 金字塔时代，持续了 300 年。 不仅仅是金字塔，狮身人面像也 是在这个时候建造的，大约在公 元前 2550 年。 今天你也可以看到金字塔和狮身 人面像，但你要记得，如今你可

paragraphs.en: that these structures are actually ruins which do not look as grand as they did thousands of years ago. Originally, the great pyramid was covered with white limestone that shone brilliantly in the sunlight, and the sphinx was painted and the stone carving had sharp distinct features. These buildings have stood under the Egyptian sun for 4500 years. We don't have time to get into all the details of Egyptian history, that would be as rich a story as the details of Chinese history, and would require many many hours to tell. But the Egyptians ruled themselves for 2500 years. This time can be divided into the Old Kingdom, the Middle Kingdom and the New
paragraphs.zh: 以看到的只有废墟了，远不及数 千年前那么辉煌。 最初，大金字塔上覆盖着白色的 石灰石，在阳光下闪耀着光芒， 狮身人面像上刻着精美的石雕， 有着鲜明的特色。 这些建筑物在埃及的阳光下闪耀 了 4500 年。 我们没有时间深入了解埃及历史 的所有细节，这将会是一个丰富 的故事，就像中国历史的细节一 样，需要很多时间来讲述。 但埃及人自治了 2500 年，这段时 间可以被分为古王朝时期、中王 朝时期和新王朝时期。三个王朝 中间分别是第一、第二、第三中

paragraphs.en: Kingdom, and in-between are the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Intermediate periods. But then the Persians conquered Egypt around 525 BC and Egypt was forever afterwards under the control of other cultures. Like the Sumerians, we know a lot about the ancient Egyptians because they also developed a writing system. Their system developed separately from the Mesopotamian system, and developed around the same time. We are not actually sure which system is the oldest. Of course the Iraqi historians like to think they are older, and the Egyptian historians disagree.
paragraphs.zh: 间阶段。 然后，波斯人在公元前 525 年征 服了埃及。之后，埃及就始终处 在其他文化的统治之下。 和苏美尔人一样，我们对古埃及 人能够有很多了解，是因为他们 也开发了一个文字系统。 大约在同一时期，埃及文字与美 索不达米亚的文字系统在不同的 地区独立发展。 我们无法确认那种体系更为古 老。当然伊拉克的历史学家认为 他们的文字更古老，而埃及历史 学家则持相反意见。

paragraphs.en: But for our purposes we'll call it a tie and say that both are equally old. The Egyptian writing system came in 3 forms: Hieroglyphics which were carved into stone for religious purposes. Hieratic, which was a kind of cursive hieroglyphics that were faster to write on papyrus. And later on came Demotic, which develop about 1500 years later, and was an even faster writing system, which was used more for daily life. That concludes our look at Egypt. Next class we are going to move north and look at what was happening in Europe around this time. Thank you very much. Okay, I hope you all enjoyed this
paragraphs.zh: 但与我们而言，我们更愿意认为 他们的历史差不多悠久。 埃及的文字系统有三种形式：象 形文字是为了宗教目的而刻在石 头上的。 祭司体，是一种草书的象形文字， 在莎草纸上写得更快。 大约 1500 年后，又出现了世俗体， 用这种体系书写速度更快，因此 更多地在日常生活中使用。 关于埃及的故事到此为止。下节 课我们将把目光向北移动，看看 这段时间欧洲发生了什么。 谢谢大家。 好的，我希望你们都喜欢这节课。

paragraphs.en: class. If you want to learn more, here are some great resources: Two important artifacts which I mentioned in this class are the Narmer Palette and the Djoser statue. These can both be seen at the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, Egypt. Another important artifact, the Imhotep Statue, can be found, like so much else, at the Louvre Museum in Paris. This is a map of modern Egypt, and this shows you where the Giza pyramids can be seen. These are just outside of Cairo, the capital city. To the south is theAswan dam, which ended the 5000-year long tradition of Nile flooding.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你想了解更多，这里有一些 很好的资源： 我在这节课上提到的两大重要工 艺品是那美尔调色板和左赛尔雕 像。 这些都可以在埃及开罗的埃及博 物馆看到。 另一件重要的艺术品：英霍蒂普 雕像，与许多其他文物一样，在 法国卢浮宫展出。 这是一张现代埃及地图，这张地 图向你展示了吉萨金字塔的位 置。这些金字塔就在首都开罗郊 外。 南部是阿斯旺大坝，它结束了长 达 5000 年的尼罗河洪水。

paragraphs.en: Finally, if you'd like to learn more about the details of Egyptian history, I want to recommend The History of Egypt Podcast by Dominic Perry. And for a general-purpose world history textbook, Voyages in World History by Valerie Hansen is also very good. All right everyone. Thank you very much.And I’ll see you next time.
paragraphs.zh: 最后，如果你想了解更多关于埃 及历史的细节，我想推荐多米尼 克·佩里的埃及历史播客。 作为一本世界历史通识教材，芮 乐伟·韩森的《世界历史航程》（非 官方译名）也很不错。 今天就到这里，谢谢大家！

titleZh: 第九讲：一直在金字塔的埃及，分分合合近 1000 年的埃及

paragraphs.en: Hello!And welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we will continue our lesson on Egypt and learn about the Old Kingdom period. Here is what the Old Kingdom looks like on a timeline.As you can see here, it began right around the start of the Bronze age and lasted for almost a millennium. The exact dates of this are a little uncertain, since our records for this period are some of the oldest historical records in the world, and they are a little fragmentary. So let's begin. So our story probably should begin around 5500 BC when knowledge of farming first reached the Nile
paragraphs.zh: 1000 年的埃及 大家好!欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列课程。今天我们将继续之前关 于埃及的内容，来学习古王国时期 的历史。 这是时间线上的古王国时期。可以 看到，它始于青铜时代的初期，持 续了将近 1000 年的时间。 确切的时间不太好说，因为关于这 一时期的记载可以说是世界上最 古老的历史记录之一了，比较零 散。 那么让我们开始吧。 所以我们的故事大约从公元前 5500 年开始，当时农耕技术首次 从中东地区传播到了尼罗河流域。

paragraphs.en: River Valley from the Middle East. Now to be honest, farming may have arrived 1000 years earlier than that, but we have good evidence that people were growing crops and raising livestock by 5500 BC, so let's start our story there. The earliest farming culture the develop in the Nile River valley is known as the Badari culture. Since we have no evidence of large migrations into Egypt after this point, we think that the Badari culture were ancestors to the ancient Egyptians. The Badari people had stone tools, but they also grew barley and wheat and kept cattle, sheep and goats. The also fished from the Nile and hunted.
paragraphs.zh: 说实话，这个时间点可能早 1000 年也说不定，但我们目前的证据能 够确定的是，公元前 5500 年左右 人们在此种植庄稼和饲养牲畜，所 以就让故事从这里开始吧。 尼罗河流域最早的农耕文化被称 为拜达里文化。 因为目前没有证据表明在此之后 有大规模的移民进入埃及，所以我 们认为拜达里人就是古埃及人的 祖先。 拜达里人使用石器，但他们也种植 大麦和小麦，饲养牛、绵羊和山羊。 他们也从尼罗河流域捕鱼、狩猎。

paragraphs.en: We have discovered at least 40 settlements and 600 graves belonging to these people, and most of our artifacts are grave goods that were buried along with people in cemeteries. These two figurines of women that you see in this slide were found in a Badari grave. Eventually Badarian culture evolved into a new culture which archaeologists call Naqada culture. The artifacts from this culture are slightly more sophisticated and much of their pottery has a distinctive black-topped design to it. As we have seen elsewhere in the world, farming caused the population to grow and by 3500 BC, there were at least 4 kingdoms along the Nile river.
paragraphs.zh: 我们已经发现了 40 个以上的定居 点和 600 余个墓穴，同时我们找到 的大多数手工艺品都是做为陪葬 品和人一同下葬的。 你们在这张图片上看到的这两个 女性雕像，就是在拜达里的墓葬里 发现的。 最终，拜达里文化演变成为一种新 的文化，考古学家称之为涅伽达文 化。 这个文化的手工艺品变得更加精 致一些，他们的大部分陶器都有独 特的黑色顶饰设计。 众所周知，农业会促使人口增长， 因此到了公元前 3500 年，尼罗河 沿岸至少出现了 4 个王国。

paragraphs.en: One of them is still called Naqada, but the others are called Nekhen, Thinis and Ta-Mehu (which was lower Egypt) The King of lower Egypt wore a distinctive red crown with a curly section called the "Deshret" and the king of Nekhen wore a white crown, that looks like a bowling ball pin to me, this was called the Hedjet. During this time, the very first Egyptian writing emerges and we begin to have names for some of these very early Kings. One of these Kings was called "Selk" or King scorpion. He ruled Nekhen around 3100 BC. Eventually, around 3000 to 2950 BC, one King became the most powerful. This King's name was
paragraphs.zh: 其中一个国家至今仍被称为涅伽 达，其他的几个则被称为尼肯、提 尼斯和塔-梅胡（即下埃及）。 下埃及的国王戴着一顶独特的红 色王冠，王冠上部有一个卷曲的部 分，被称为“红冠”，而尼肯的国 王戴着一顶白色的王冠，在我看来 有点像一个保龄球瓶，它叫做白 冠。 在此期间，早期的埃及文字诞生 了，当时的这些国王开始有了称 号。 其中一位国王被称为“塞尔克”或 蝎子王。他在公元前 3100 年左右 统治了尼肯。 终于，在约公元前 3000-2950 年， 纳尔迈成为了最强大的国王。我们 认为他来自尼肯。他迎娶了提尼斯

paragraphs.en: Narmer, and we think he was the King of Nekhen. He married Neithhotep, the Queen of Thinis, and then together they attacked Naqada from both sides and defeated it. Then the combined forces of Nekhen, Naqada and Thinis conquered lower Egypt, and the whole country was unified for the first time. Therefore, it is Narmer who became the first King of a unified Egypt. However, this happened so long ago, at the very beginning of written history. So our evidence is fragmentary, and much of it comes from stories that were written hundreds of years after Narmer died. So Narmer, like Sargon of Akkad, is a figure who might be
paragraphs.zh: 女王尼特霍特普，两人共同夹击涅 伽达并取得了胜利。 接下来，尼肯、涅伽达和提尼斯三 股势力联合征服了下埃及，整个国 家首次迎来了统一。 因此，纳尔迈成为统一埃及的第一 任国王。 然而，这件事发生在很久以前，属 于最古老的有记载的历史。所以我 们能找到的史证是支离破碎的，其 中大部分故事记载于纳尔迈死后 几百年。所以纳尔迈可能就像阿卡 德的萨尔贡一样，是一个真实存在 但带有传奇色彩的历史人物。

paragraphs.en: part historical, part legendary. But we will begin the story of Egypt with him. One of the most important artifacts that we have from this time period is the Narmer Palette. This is a stone carving which depicts Narmer's conquest of Egypt, and it was made around 3000 BC, so it was created during his life-time. This stone carving was the evidence that convinced historians that Narmer was probably a real king. It also has some of the oldest hieroglyphics ever found. Historian Bob Brier has called the Narmer Palette: "the oldest historical document in the world." On one side of the Palette you can
paragraphs.zh: 但埃及的故事，将从他开始。 纳尔迈调色板是这个时期最重要 的文物之一。 这是一件描绘纳尔迈征服埃及的 石雕作品，制作于公元前 3000 年 左右，因此这件物品创作于纳尔迈 所在的时代。正是这一件石雕让历 史学家确信了纳尔迈的真实性。 石雕上还有一些最古老的象形文 字。历史学家鲍勃·布里尔称纳尔 迈调色板为“世界上最古老的历史 文献”。 在调色板的一面，你可以看到纳尔

paragraphs.en: see Narmer, killing his enemies while wearing the Hedjet crown, showing that he was King of Upper Egypt. The God Ra looks down upon all this with approval. Then the other side shows Narmer wearing the crown of southern Egypt, the Deshret, and also ruling over everything there. By showing Narmer wearing both crowns, the Palette is telling us that he was the King of all Egypt. Unfortunately, King or not, Narmer was mortal and after uniting all Egypt, he was killed by a Hippopotamus! You might think this is funny, but Hippos are actually the most dangerous animal inAfrica, each year they kill many more people than crocodiles hyenas and lions.
paragraphs.zh: 迈头戴红冠杀敌的场景，表明他是 上埃及的国王。太阳神正带着赞许 的目光俯视着这一切。 另一面则是纳尔迈戴着埃及南部 的王冠——白冠——在当地统治 的场景。通过文物上纳尔迈戴着两 个王冠可以看出，他是整个埃及的 国王。 不幸的是，即使位至国王，纳尔迈 也是凡人，在统一了整个埃及之 后，他最后居然死于一只河马之 口! 你可能觉得这很可笑，但实际上河 马是非洲最危险的动物，每年因河 马而死的人比鳄鱼、鬣狗和狮子还 要多得多。

paragraphs.en: And 5000 years ago one of their victims was the first king of Egypt! Narmer was the founder of the 1st Dynasty of Egypt. There were 8 Kings in this first Dynasty, and I have included their names and order of their succession here. The exact dates for these Kings are hard to know, but we think they reigned mainly in the 2900s and 2800s BC. King Den was significant, because he began the practice of combining the Deshret, the crown of lower Egypt with the Hedjet, the crown of upper Egypt, into the Pschent, the unified crown of both Kingdoms. Then in the 2800s and 2700s BC, Egypt entered into the 2nd Dynasty with the reign of
paragraphs.zh: 谁能想到在 5000 年前，埃及的第 一位国王居然也是他们的受害者 之一! 受这次喷发影响最强烈的地区无 纳尔迈是埃及第一王朝的建立者。 第一王朝有 8 任国王，我在这里列 出了他们的名字和继承顺序。 这些国王统治的确切日期已经很 难追溯，但我们认为他们主要统治 于公元前 30 世纪和 29 世纪。 国王丹将代表下埃及和上埃及王 权的红冠和白冠合二为一，成为代 表统一王国王权的双重王冠，对历 史有着深远的影响。 接下来在公元前 29 世纪和 28 世 纪，随着霍-狄-锡-阿姆-怀这几位 国王的统治，埃及进入了第二王

paragraphs.en: Ho-Tep-Sek-Am-Wy. The second dynasty is even more mysterious that the 1st dynasty, and the exact order of the Kings and years that they reigned are not fully understood. But here I have given you a reasonable guess. The Kings of the 1st and 2nd Dynasty continued the Egyptian tradition of elaborate burials, and they were buried in underground chambers with a stone tomb built on top. This tomb was called a Mastaba. Then in the 2600s BC, the 2nd Dynasty ended and the 3rd Dynasty began. The first King of the 3rd Dynasty was named Djoser, and he was a powerful ruler.
paragraphs.zh: 朝。 第二王朝甚至比第一个王朝更为 神秘，我们还不能弄清楚国王继位 的确切顺序和他们统治的年代。但 在这里我给出了一种合理的猜测。 第一和第二王朝的国王们延续了 埃及人建造精致墓葬的传统，他们 被埋葬在地下的墓室里，上方盖有 一座石棺。 这种坟墓被称为石室坟墓。 接下来在公元前 27 世纪，第二王 朝结束，第三王朝开始。 第三王朝的第一任国王叫做左塞 尔，他是一个强大的统治者。

paragraphs.en: Djoser conquered the Sinai peninsula, and gained control of valuable turquoise and copper mines. But Djoser is most famous for building the first pyramid. This was a series of Mastabas, each built on top of the other which get smaller as you go up. This was the Step Pyramid, and it was the very first Pyramid. Djoser's idea of a Pyramid worked so well, that all of his successors copied him and started building pyramids of their own. There were five Kings in the 3rd Dynasty, and we think that they all tried building pyramids. Unfortunately, there were not all successful. Djoserty, the 2nd King,
paragraphs.zh: 左塞尔征服了西奈半岛，从而掌握 了珍贵的绿松石和铜矿的控制权。 但最让左塞尔闻名于世的还是建 造了第一座金字塔。 金字塔由许多个石室坟墓组成，层 层叠加，越往上石棺会越来越小。 这就是阶梯金字塔，它也是最早期 的金字塔。 左塞尔关于金字塔的想法非常成 功，以至于他的继任者们都纷纷效 仿，开始建造自己的金字塔。 在第三王朝有五个国王，我们认为 他们都试图建造金字塔。 不幸的是，并不是每个人都成功 了。第二任国王乔赛蒂的金字塔，

paragraphs.en: was unable to finish his pyramid before he died. So it is called the "Buried pyramid. We don't know where Nebka the 3rd King is buried. Teti, the 4th King did build a pyramid, which today is a ruin called the "Layer Pyramid" and Huni the 5th King built a pyramid at Meidum which still stands today. After Huni came the 4th Dynasty of Egypt.And it was these strong Kings who began the GoldenAge of pyramid building. The first King in this line was Sneferu who built 2 pyramids. These were the first true pyramids. Then came Khufu who built the Great Pyramid, and then Djedefre. Djedefre's pyramid was destroyed by the Romans who used its stones to build other things nearby.
paragraphs.zh: 在他死前也没能建造完成。所以它 被称为“被埋葬的金字塔”。我们 不知道第三任国王尼布卡葬在哪 里。第四位国王泰提确实建造了一 座金字塔，但今天已经成为了叫做 “层级金字塔”的废墟，第五位国 王胡尼在美杜姆建造的金字塔，至 今仍屹立不倒。 胡尼之后便是埃及的第四王朝。这 些强大的国王开启了金字塔建筑 的黄金时代。 这其中的第一任国王是建造了两 座金字塔的斯奈弗鲁。它们是第一 批真正意义上的金字塔。 接着是修建了大金字塔的胡夫，然 后是杰德弗雷。为了使用金字塔上 的石材在附近建造其他建筑，杰德 弗雷的金字塔被罗马人拆毁了。

paragraphs.en: Then came the 4th King Khafre, who built the Pyramid of Khafre and the Sphinx. Then came Menkaure who build the smaller Pyramid of Menkaure, this was deliberately damaged by a later muslim ruler, and finally came Shepsekaf who did not rule long enough to build a pyramid and only has a Mastaba. Sneferu, the 1st King of the 4th Dynasty, began the golden age of Pyramid building, and also built the first true pyramids. While building the 1st one, his architect realized that it was too steep and changed the angle half-way through building it, so this one is called the "Bent Pyramid." Sneferu's 2nd pyramid is called the Red Pyramid, because the outer
paragraphs.zh: 然后是第四任国王哈夫拉，他建造 了哈夫拉金字塔和斯芬克斯金字 塔。 接着是蒙卡乌尔，他建造了更小的 门卡乌尔金字塔，但被后来的一位 穆斯林统治者故意破坏了，最后是 谢普谢克夫，他的统治时间不足以 修建金字塔，因此只有一座石室陵 墓。 第四王朝的首任国王斯奈弗鲁开 启了金字塔建筑的黄金时代，也建 造了第一个真正意义上的金字塔。 在建造第一个金字塔时，他的建筑 师发现由于坡面太陡，金字塔在建 造的过程中角度发生了改变，因此 这个金字塔被称为“弯曲金字塔”。 斯奈弗鲁的第二个金字塔被称为 红色金字塔，因为外部的白色石头

paragraphs.en: white stones were all looted by later people and only the interior red stones remain. This Red Pyramid is the oldest true pyramid. And then came Khufu, the 2nd King of the 4th Dynasty, and he built the largest Pyramid of all, the Great Pyramid. The Great Pyramid was completed around 2570 BC, and it is 147 meters tall. Once it was finished it became the tallest building on Earth.And it remained the tallest building on Earth for almost 4000 years. Its height was not surpassed until 1311AD, when the tower of Lincoln Cathedral is England was finished.
paragraphs.zh: 都被后人掠夺一空，只剩下了里层 的红色石头。 这个红色金字塔是最古老的真正 金字塔。 然后是第四王朝的第二任国王胡 夫，他建造的金字塔是最大的，因 此被称为大金字塔。 大金字塔建成于公元前 2570 年左 右，高 147 米。 建成后，它成为了当时地球上最高 的建筑，并在将近 4000 年的时间 里，一直保持这项纪录。 它的高度记录直到公元 1311 年， 英国林肯大教堂的高塔完工时才 被超越。

paragraphs.en: Of course these pyramids were massive construction projects. So I'd like to take a moment to talk a little about the people who built them. The pyramid builders were divided up into teams of 20 men, and each team would have a team leader. 10 teams would make a phile, which had 200 men. 5 philes would make a gang of 1000 men. A2 gangs would make a crew of 2000 men. Typically, 2 or 3 crews would work during the flood season to build the pyramid. Modern estimates tell us that it took about 4000 to 6000 workers 20 years to build the great Pyramid.
paragraphs.zh: 这些金字塔可以说是超大规模的 建筑工程。所以我想谈谈建造它们 的人。 金字塔的建造者分为 20 人一组， 每个小组有一个组长。 10 个小组组成一个腓利，共有 200 人。 5 个腓利可以组成一个 1000 人的 群组。 两个群组可以组成 2000 人的小 队。 通常来说，2 到 3 个群组会在汛期 开展建造金字塔的工作。 通过现在的推算，建造大金字塔使 用了大约 4000 到 6000 名工人，花 了 20 年的时间。

paragraphs.en: But those 6000 workers are just the direct builders. People were also needed to feed those workers, cook for them, provide medical care for injuries, transport materials, etc. It is estimated that these 6000 workers had a support staff of about 16,000 people who kept them productive. Therefore, the pyramids represent the labor of 20,000 people working for 4 months a year, across 20 years. There is a common perception that the pyramids were built by slaves. This probably comes from the Bible stories of the Jews being slaves in Egypt. It has also been reinforced by Hollywood. But the slave story is probably untrue. The pyramid builders received medical care it they were injured.
paragraphs.zh: 但这 6000 名工人只是直接参与的 建设者。工程还需要更多人力为这 些工人提供食物，做饭，为受伤人 员提供医疗照顾，运输材料等等。 据估计，大约有 1.6 万人的保障人 员为这 6000 名工人服务，以提高 工人的工作效率。 因此，金字塔代表了 2 万人每年工 作 4 个月，历时 20 年的劳动成果。 人们普遍认为金字塔是由奴隶建 造的。这可能来自与圣经中犹太人 在埃及做奴隶的故事。好莱坞电影 也加深了人们的这一印象。但是奴 隶的故事可能并不真实。 金字塔建造者们如果受伤，会受到 治疗。我们之所以知道这一点，是

paragraphs.en: We know this because we have found the skeletons of some of the builders. Some of them have broken bones that were then expertly set and healed properly. This is strong evidence that the builders received good medical care. We also know that builders were fed meat every day. The King slaughtered at least 20 cattle a day to feed his workers. Meat was a rare luxury in the ancient world. This is not the sort of thing you feed slaves. And finally, the pyramid builders would tag their work with graffiti. For example, we have found this painted on a stone inside the Great Pyramid. It says "Work Gang: the Friends of Khufu". Rather than being slaves, we think the pyramid builders were intensely
paragraphs.zh: 因为我们发现了一些建筑工人的 骨架。他们中有些人的骨头断了， 但是经过专业的治疗后伤愈了。这 有力地证明了工人得到了良好的 医疗照顾。 我们也知道建筑工人每天都有肉 吃。国王每天至少屠宰 20 头牛提 供给他的工人。肉在古代是一种稀 有的奢侈品，这不是奴隶能吃的东 西。 最后，金字塔建造者会用涂鸦来标 记他们的作品。例如，我们在大金 字塔内的一块石头上发现了这幅 画。其大意是 “工人：胡夫的朋 友”。 我们认为金字塔的建造者们对他 们的工作非常自豪，不同的团队之

paragraphs.en: proud of their work and the different teams competed with each other to see who could build faster or better. And the best teams were rewarded with extra pay and food. It was competition, good pay, and pride which motivated the pyramid workers, not slavery or coercion. After the Great Pyramid, the second most famous is probably the Pyramid of Kafra, built by Khufu's successor. You can identify this pyramid because it still has some of its outer stones around the top. The Pyramid of Khafra is also famous, because it is the pyramid in the background of all those pictures of the Sphinx. Look at the statue of Kafra on the
paragraphs.zh: 间相互竞争，比较谁能建造得更快 更好，最好的团队会得到额外的报 酬和食物。这绝不是奴隶做的事。 激励金字塔工人的是竞争、丰厚的 报酬和自豪感，而不是奴役或胁 迫。 名气仅次于大金字塔的，可能就是 由胡夫的继任者建造的这座哈夫 拉金字塔了。 你可以通过它顶部的仅存的一些 外层的石头来辨认这座金字塔。 哈夫拉金字塔作为这些狮身人面 像照片的背景，也非常有名。 看看左边哈夫拉的雕像，再看看右

paragraphs.en: left, now look at the face of the Sphinx, do you see it? The face of the Sphinx is Khafra's face. The 4th Dynasty was the golden age of Pyramid building. After them came the 5th Dynasty, which began with the rule of Userkaf. The Kings of the 5th Dynasty also built Pyramids, but they were much smaller and the quality was not as good. You can see the waning power of the Kings in the ruins of their tombs. There were 9 Kings in the 5th dynasty, but none of them could build Pyramids that compared with those built by the 4th Dynasty.
paragraphs.zh: 边的狮身人面像，看出来了吗? 狮身人面像的脸正是哈夫拉的脸。 第四王朝是金字塔建筑的黄金时 代。 在此之后是第五王朝，始于乌塞尔 卡夫的统治。 第五王朝的国王也建造了金字塔， 但它们要小得多，质量也不那么 好。 你可以在国王坟墓的废墟中，看出 王朝的力量逐渐衰弱。 第五王朝有 9 任国王，但是没有一 任国王建造的金字塔能与第四王 朝相媲美。

paragraphs.en: And then in the 2300s, the Old Kingdom of Egypt was ruled by its 6th Dynasty of Kings. There were five kings in this dynasty, and the last was named Pepi the II. Pepi was a boy King. He came to the throne when he was only 6 years old. Unfortunately, Pepi was also a very weak King. And during Pepe's lifetime, the King's lost power, while ambitious governors took more and more power for themselves and their family. Unfortunately, Pepi also lived for a long time, well into his 90s, which meant that he blocked more capable people from becoming King and
paragraphs.zh: 到公元前 24 世纪，古埃及王国由 第六王朝的国王统治。 这个王朝有五位国王，最后一位被 称为佩比二世。 佩比当时还是个少年，在六岁时就 登上了王位。 不幸的是，佩比也是一个软弱的国 王。 在佩比的一生中，国王失去了权 力，而野心勃勃的地方首领为他们 自身和家族的利益从国王手里夺 取了越来越多的权力。 佩比活了很长时间，一直到 90 多 岁，这意味着很多有能力解决问题 的人不能登上王位。

paragraphs.en: fixing the problem. Under his rule, Egypt slid into disunity and Civil War. Once Pepi II died, the governors and powerful families began to fight each other for control. and the Old Kingdom of Egypt came to an end. This period of Disunity and Civil war lasted for nearly 200 years, and it is called the 1st Intermediate Period in Egyptian History. But it did not last forever, in the 21st century BC, a strong leader would re-unite the Kingdom and begin the Middle Kingdom period of Egyptian history. However, That story will have to wait until later in the class. Now it is time for us to leave Egypt behind and begin looking at what
paragraphs.zh: 在他的任内，埃及陷入了分裂和内 战。 佩比二世死后，地方首领和权势家 族开始互相夺权。古埃及王国也随 之灭亡。 分裂和内战持续了近 200 年，这段 时期被称为埃及历史上的第一中 间期。 但天下大势，分久必合。到了公元 前 21 世纪，一个强大的领导人将 重新统一王国，并揭开埃及历史上 的中央王国时期的序幕。 然而，这个故事将留给后面的课程 了。 现在是时候让我们离开埃及，去世 界的其他地方看看了。

paragraphs.en: was happening in the rest of the world. Please join me next time as were shift our attention northwards and learn about what was happening to Europe. Thank you very much! I hope you enjoyed this lesson on the Old Kingdom of Egypt. I certainly enjoyed making it and understanding the people who made the famous Pyramids. If you'd like to learn more I have some recommendations for you. I found two good websites: The first is an article by the BBC, you can follow the link to it here. And the second is a documentary series by theAmerican Public
paragraphs.zh: 下次请和我一起把视线转向北方， 了解一下欧洲发生了什么。 谢谢大家！ 我希望你们喜欢这节关于古埃及 王国的课程。我非常喜欢这段历 史，也很理解金字塔的建造者。 如果你想深入学习，我可以给你一 些建议。 我发现了两个不错的网址: 首先是 BBC 的一篇文章，你可以点 击这里的链接。 第二个是美国公共广播公司 PBS 制作的系列纪录片，片中尝试对

paragraphs.en: Broadcaster PBS, which also tries to answer the question of Who Built the Pyramids. All right everyone. Thank you very much. And I’ll see you next time.
paragraphs.zh: “谁建造了金字塔？”这个问题给 出了回答。 今天就到这里，谢谢大家！

paragraphs.en: Hello, and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Up till now our story has been focused on ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt. The people who lived here spoke languages that belonged to the Semitic orAfro-Asiatic language families. But today nearly half of people on Earth speak languages that belong to another language family: Indo European. We can go no further in our study of world history until we learn about these people. So today we are going to leave the Middle East behind, and turn our attention to Europe. Today we will be discussing a
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列课 程。 到目前为止，我们的故事一直集中 在古代的美索不达米亚和埃及。住 在这里的人说的是属于闪族或亚 非语系的语言。 但如今，地球上几乎有一半的人都 在使用属于另一个语系的语言：印 欧语系。 如果不了解这些人，我们就无法更 深入地学习世界历史了。 所以今天我们要把中东放在一边， 把注意力转向欧洲。 今天我们将讨论一个大约持续

paragraphs.en: period of about 2000 years, from 5000 to 3000 BC, so here is what that looks like on our large 12,000-year timeline. Today is part 1 in a 2-part story about a people we call the Indo Europeans. These people were very important to world history, because their language proto-Indo-european is the ancestor language to languages which half of the world speaks today. They are also the ancestor people to the Celts, Greeks, Hittites, Persians, Aryans, Germans, Russians and several other people who will play a big role in our history. If you remember the lesson on Neolithic Europe, you might recall that farming was invented in the Middle East and then spread to
paragraphs.zh: 2000 年的时期，从公元前 5000- 3000 年，在我们长达 12,000 年的 时间线上，这段时间是这样的。 关于印欧人，我们将分2节课来讲。 今天是第一节。这些人在世界历史 中占有非常重要的位置，因为他们 的语言：原始印欧语，是如今世界 上一半语言的源头。 他们也是凯尔特人、希腊人、赫梯 人、波斯人、雅利安人、德国人、 俄罗斯人和其他一些人的祖先，而 这些人都在我们的历史中扮演着 重要角色。 如果你还记得关于新石器时代欧 洲的那节课，你可能会记得，农业 发源自中东，然后从公元前 7000 年开始传播到欧洲。最初，它蔓延

paragraphs.en: Europe beginning around 7000 BC. First it spread to Crete, theAegean, and the Peloponnesian Peninsula. Then it spread throughout the Balkans and the eastern coast of the Italian Peninsula. It also reached the interior of Europe by travelling down the Danube river, and throughout the Hungarian plain.All this had happened by about 6000 BC. But then the spread ofAgriculture was delayed for several centuries. These first farmers of Europe were unused to the harsh winters and thick soil of northern Europe.And farming stopped its spread for almost a thousand years. The solution to this problem was the plow, which allowed these first farmers of Europe to efficiently
paragraphs.zh: 到了克里特岛、爱琴海和伯罗奔尼 撒半岛。 然后它遍布了整个巴尔干半岛和 意大利半岛的东部海岸。它还沿着 多瑙河贯穿了整个匈牙利平原，到 达欧洲内陆。所有这一切都发生在 公元前 6000 年左右。 但随后，农业的扩张推迟了几个世 纪。欧洲的初代农民不习惯欧洲的 严冬和厚土。近千年间，农业停止 了传播。 解决这个问题的方法是犁，这使得 欧洲的初代农民能够有效地分解 欧洲的厚土。

paragraphs.en: break up the thick European soils. And the other solution was an improved knowledge of the calendar, which allowed farmers to more accurately predict the arrival of the seasons and the proper timing of the planting season and harvest season. It may also have taken time for these First Farmers of Europe to develop crop strains that were better adapted to the European climate. The Europeans were still hunter-gatherers when Middle-Eastern farmers first arrived. But the two groups intermarried and formed the ancestors to most modern Europeans. Farming came to Europe from the Middle East, because Europe did
paragraphs.zh: 另一种解决方案是对历法的改进， 使农民能够更准确地预测季节更 替，以及种植和收获的适当时机。 欧洲初代农民或许还花了很长时 间来开发适合欧洲气候的作物。 中东农民最初来到这里时，欧洲人 仍然靠狩猎采集为生。但这两个群 体开始通婚，并形成了大多数现代 欧洲人的祖先。 农业从中东来到欧洲，因为欧洲没 有很多适合驯化的植物。

paragraphs.en: not have many plants that were suitable for domestication. These very first crops were Emmer Wheat, Einkorn wheat, Barley, Lentils, Peas, Chickpeas, Bitter Vetch and Flax. Together these crops are known as the "Neolithic Founder Crops" because they laid the foundation for farming. But the Middle-Eastern farmers did not just bring plants, they also brought animals. Now the Europeans, already had the dog, but all of the other animals also came from the middle east: These animals were Goats, Pigs, Sheep, Cows, Cats and Chickens. We can call them the "Neolithic FounderAnimals" because, along
paragraphs.zh: 这些最早的农作物是小麦，大麦， 扁豆，豌豆，鹰嘴豆，苦野豌豆和 亚麻。 这些作物被称为“新石器时代的奠 基作物”，因为它们为农业奠定了 基础。 但是，中东的农民不仅带来了植 物，还带来了动物。 欧洲人已经有了狗，但是其他的动 物也都来自中东： 山羊、猪、羊、牛、猫和鸡。 我们可以称它们为“新石器时代的 奠基动物”，因为与农作物一起，

paragraphs.en: with the crops, these animals were the other half of the foundation for agriculture. As we humans domesticated animals, we selected for certain genes. This caused the animals to change. Perhaps one of the most important changes is what happened to the sheep. Sheep are originally a Middle Eastern animal, and so in the Middle East they have very short coats, very much like goats. But when sheep moved to Europe they changed. Maybe this happened because of Europe's colder weather, but some sheep developed a mutation that allowed them to grow longer, woolly coats.
paragraphs.zh: 这些动物是农业基础的另一半。 当人类驯化动物时，我们对其进行 了某些基因筛选。这导致了动物的 改变。 最重要的变化发生在绵羊身上。 绵羊最初是中东动物，所以在中 东，它们的皮毛很短，很像山羊。 但是，当绵羊迁移到欧洲时，它们 发生了变化。也许是因为欧洲寒冷 的天气，一些绵羊发生了突变：它 们长出了更长的绒毛。

paragraphs.en: This was a big change because it meant that sheep could now become a source of wool. Originally, sheep were probably domesticated for their meat, hides and milk, just like cows. But once sheep grew long fur, humans could collect their wool without having to kill the animal. This meant that sheep could now give humans an additional product, and they became much more valuable! People took advantage of this new product by learning how to shear sheep to collect their wool in the spring. Then people learned how to spin the wool into thread and weave this thread into cloth. Actually people probably had some of these skills, because flax, one of
paragraphs.zh: 这是一个重大变化，因为这意味着 羊可以成为羊毛的来源。最初，羊 很可能是因为肉、皮和奶而被驯化 的，就像牛一样。 但是，一旦绵羊长了长长的绒毛， 人类收集羊毛时就可以不用杀死 动物了。这意味着绵羊可以为人类 提供额外的产品，它们变得更有价 值了！ 人们利用这个新产品，学会了如何 在春天修剪并收集羊毛。然后，人 们学会了如何将羊毛纺成线，并将 这些线编织成布料。 实际上，当时的人类很可能具备这 样的技能。因为新石器奠基作物之

paragraphs.en: the Neolithic founder crops, can be spun into Linen. But now humans had three different materials for clothes, furs, wool, and linen. The development of wool clothing probably made life in cold northern Europe much easier, and may have helped the development of society in that part of the world. After 5000 BC, farming and animal husbandry spread all across Europe. But today I want to focus on a region north of the Black Sea. This region contains the river valleys for the Dniester and Boh rivers.And between 5000 and 3000 BC, a culture developed here known as the Cucuteni-Trypillia culture. These people practiced farming, raised animals, and built large settlements just like the
paragraphs.zh: 一的亚麻，就可以被纺织成亚麻 布。 现在，人类有三种不同的材料可用 于制衣：皮革，羊毛和亚麻布。羊 毛服装的发展可能使北欧寒冷的 生活变得更加容易，并且可能帮助 了这个地区的社会发展。 公元前 5000 年之后，农业和畜牧 业遍布整个欧洲。但今天我想把重 点放在黑海北部的一个地区。 这一地区包含了德涅斯特河和博 河的河谷。在公元前 5000-3000 年 之间，这里形成了一种被称为“库 库泰尼-特里波耶”的文化。 就像美索不达米亚人一样，这里的 人们也从事农业，饲养动物，建造 了大型定居点。

paragraphs.en: Mesopotamians. And this really shouldn't be a surprise.As you can see from this picture, the Dniester river valley is a great place for a farm. The soil is rich and productive. Additionally, the river makes transportation and trade easier. So once more we see a historical pattern: once farming becomes developed, and the population grows enough, advanced societies start to develop along the River Valley. River Valleys are the cradles of civilization. The Trypillians developed into a very sophisticated society between 5000 and 3000 BC. Their villages grew into towns and
paragraphs.zh: 这不能算是一个惊喜。从这幅图中 可以看出，德涅斯特河流域是一个 发展农业的好地方。这里的土壤肥 沃多产。 此外，这条河还有利于交通和贸易 的发展。 因此，我们再一次看到了一种历史 模式：一旦农业发展起来，人口增 长到一定程度，先进的社会就会沿 着河谷发展起来。 河谷是文明的摇篮。 到了公元前 5000-3000 年的时候， 这些人逐渐形成了一个非常复杂 的社会。 他们的村庄发展成小镇，小镇发展

paragraphs.en: their towns grew into small cities. Some of these settlements like Talianki can be compared to Mesopotamian cities like Uruk or Lagash. The Trypillians also built beautiful houses and developed advanced pottery. Here is a reconstruction of a Trypillian house. Notice the vibrant, reds, oranges and whites, and the decorations painted on the outside walls. The walls were made using wattle and daub construction. The frame was made out of wooden beams. Then the roof was covered with sticks and with thatch, made either from reeds or grass stems. Here is a cut-away of a wattle and
paragraphs.zh: 成小城市。 一些像塔连基这样的定居点可以 拿来和像乌鲁克或拉加什这样的 美索不达米亚城市进行比较。 同时，塔连基人也建造了漂亮的房 子，并且制造了先进的陶器。 这是一座重建的塔连基房屋。请注 意看那些鲜艳的红色、橙色、白色 和外墙的装饰。 墙壁是用“抹灰篱笆墙”结构制成 的。框架是用木梁做成的。 屋顶上还覆盖着树枝和茅草，可能 是芦苇，或是草茎。 这是一个用抹灰篱笆墙技术制成

paragraphs.en: daub house. It is a reconstruction of a Trypillian house which also shows us how the house was built. The house was built out of a wooden frame. The spaces in-between the posts would be filled in with long interwoven sticks. This is called a "wattle." Then the walls would be covered with mud or sometimes plaster. This is called "daub." So together this construction technique is known as "wattle and daub" and it has been used in Europe for thousands of years. But Trypillian houses also have another special feature. Every 80 years, it seems that the Trypillians would take everything out of the house and burn it down on purpose. Then they would smooth over the
paragraphs.zh: 的房屋的剖开立体图，这是现代人 对房子的重建，它也向我们展示了 这座房子是如何建成的。 这栋房子是由木头框架建成的，结 构中间的空间用互相交织的长木 棍填满，这就是所谓的“篱笆”。 墙面会涂满泥土，有时还会抹石 膏。这就是所谓的“抹灰”。因此， 这种技术被称为“抹灰篱笆墙”， 在欧洲已经使用了数千年。 但是塔连基人的房子还有另一个 特点。每隔 80 年，他们就会将房 屋内的所有物品取出，故意烧毁房 屋。 然后他们收拾地面，再在原处重建

paragraphs.en: ground and re-build. Why they did this is still something of a mystery. Some people think they did this as part of their religious beliefs. They thought that house have a life cycle of birth and death.And that life cycle is equal to a long human life. Or perhaps a house made of wood and mud just does not last much longer than 80 years, and it makes more sense to burn it down and rebuild than to keep trying to fix it once the wood is rotten. In any case, house-burning is one of the distinctive features of Trypillian culture, and archaeologists have even called this culture "The Burned-House Horizon" for that reason.
paragraphs.zh: 房屋。 至于他们为什么这么做，至今仍是 一个谜。 有些人认为他们这样做，是他们宗 教信仰的一部分：房子也有生与死 的生命周期，该周期等于人类的寿 命。 又或者，一所由木头和泥土制成的 房子，不能持续使用超过 80 年， 而且如果木头腐烂了，把它烧掉并 重建比继续修复要更合适。 总之，烧房子是塔连基文化的特色 之一，考古学家甚至因此将这种文 化称为“烧屋文化（非官方译名）”。

paragraphs.en: In any case, the Trypillians developed quite a sophisticated culture beginning about 7000 years ago.As you can see they developed excellent wool clothing, which made life along the northern coast of the Black Sea more comfortable. And they made beautiful pottery. We have also found metal Trypillian artifacts, but we don't think they relied on local sources of copper. Instead these were probably prized luxury items that were traded hundreds of kilometers. But the real big impact on history did not come from the Trypillians. It came from their neighbors. Archaeologists call these people the Yamna, and we think they are the people who spoke the original Indo-European language, which linguists refer to as "Proto
paragraphs.zh: 总而言之，在大约 7000 年前，塔 连基人发展出了一种复杂的文化。 正如你所看到的，他们发明了优秀 的羊毛服装，这使得黑海北部海岸 的生活更加舒适。他们制作了漂亮 的陶器。我们还发现了金属制成的 塔连基手工艺品，但我们认为它们 并不是由当地铜资源制成。相反， 这些东西可能是被交易了数百公 里的珍贵奢侈品。 但是，真正对历史的重大影响并非 来自塔连基人，而是他们的邻居。 考古学家称这些人为亚穆纳人，他 们使用原始印欧语，语言学家称之 为“原型印欧语”。

paragraphs.en: Indo-European" Between 5000 and 3000 BC, these people would change in a few profound ways. And this change would allow them to live in a brand new way, which humans had never before experienced. This change is almost as important as the invention of farming. So please join me next time as we learn about the Horse, the Wheel, and the Language. Thank you very much. I hope you guys found this stuff about the Trypillian-Cucuteni culture as interesting as I do. I was looking for resources to share with you if you want to learn more, but unfortunately, the Chinese and English language resources on this
paragraphs.zh: 在公元前 5000-3000 年之间，他们 的改变意义深远。这种改变将使他 们能够以一种全新的方式生活，这 是人类以前从未经历过的。 这种变化几乎和农业的发明一样 重要。所以下节课，请和我一起学 习马，轮子和语言。 谢谢大家。 希望你们也喜欢库库泰尼-特里波 耶文化。 如果你想了解更多，我正在寻找资 源与你分享，但不幸的是，关于库 库泰尼-特里波耶文化，中文和英 文资源仍然相当有限。关于这种文

paragraphs.en: culture are still pretty limited. Most of the history about this culture is limited to academic journals or its written in Russian. But I did find some think if you'd like to explore further. The first is a museum in Northern Romania in the town of Piatra Neamt. Most of the pictures that I showed you for Cucuteni artifacts are held in that museum. But there is another museum is Cherkasky Ukraine. This is called the Trypillian culture museum. The photo of a reconstructed Trypillian house is located there. There is another great YouTube channel called How to Make Everything.
paragraphs.zh: 化的大部分历史都局限于学术期 刊，或是以俄语写成的。 但如果你想了解更多，我还是可以 提供一些思路。 首先是位于罗马尼亚北部的皮亚 特拉尼亚姆茨的一个博物馆。 我展示的大部分照片都来自这个 博物馆。 另一个是位于乌克兰切尔卡斯基 的特里波耶文化博物馆。重建的塔 连基房屋就位于这个博物馆。 还有一个很棒的油管频道，名为

paragraphs.en: In this channel the presenterAndy George makes all sorts of tools and clothes from scratch and shows you the entire process. He makes a shirt out of wool, which I recommend if you want to get a detailed understanding of how wool is really made. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 在这个频道里，主持人安迪乔治会 从头开始制作各种各样的工具和 衣服，并向你展示整个过程。 而欧洲人带有的尼安德特基因最 多，因为尼安德特人主要就生活在 欧洲。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第十一讲：古印欧是如何成为世界上 50%语言源头的？

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on world history. Today we are going to continue talking about the same part of the world: the North coast of the Black Sea. But we are going to shift our focus a little and discuss a new group of people who changed the world: The Indo-Europeans. In terms of our timeline, we will be looking at the period between 5000 and 2000 BC. So we are looking at 3000 years of history. This is what that looks like on our big 12,000-year timeline. Last time, I taught you about the
paragraphs.zh: 50%语言源头的？ 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列课程。 今天我们将继续讨论黑海北岸的 历史。 但是我们要将目光对准另一个新 群体，一群改变了世界的人：印欧 人。 根据我们的时间线，我们要关注的 是公元前 5000-2000 年。 所以我们将了解一段跨越了 3000 年的历史。 在我们涵盖了 12,000 年的大时间 线上，这 3000 年看起来是这样的。 上一讲，我讲到了特里波耶文化。

paragraphs.en: Trypillian culture, which developed farming and large settlements along the Dniester, Boh and Dnieper river valleys, which today are located in modern Ukraine. These people developed sophisticated pottery, large towns, and woolen clothes. They also had an interesting tradition of burning their houses every few decades, perhaps for reasons of hygiene, or because of religious beliefs. But today I want to shift our attention away from the Trypillians, and a little further east towards their neighbors. Trypillian culture is interesting, because if we put its location on a map of Europe, we can see that the Trypillians lived north of the Black
paragraphs.zh: 该文化沿着德涅斯特河、博河，以 及第聂伯河河谷发展出了农耕和 大型聚居群落，也就是今天的乌克 兰地区。 这些人发展出了复杂的诗歌、大型 城镇，还会做羊毛服装。 他们还有一个有趣的传统，每过几 十年就会把房屋烧掉。可能是出于 卫生的原因，也可能是由于宗教信 仰。 但是今天我们要关注的不是特里 波耶，而是在他们东边不远的邻 居。 特里波耶文化十分有趣，因为如果 我们我们从欧洲地图上观察它的 位置，会发现它位于黑海北面、两 种不同的生态区：温带森林和干草

paragraphs.en: Sea, at a place where two different kinds of ecosystems meet: The temperate forests and the Steppe Grasslands. On this slide I have included a picture from both types of ecosystems. The forests covered almost all of Europe at this time. These were ancient old growth forests, which had been growing since the end of the ice ages. The western part of Trypillian territory was covered with such forests. But to the east, the climate becomes much more dry. Here it is too dry for forests to grow, but still wet enough for grasslands.
paragraphs.zh: 原的交汇处。 这张图片展示了这两种生态区的 风貌。 此时，森林覆盖了欧洲大陆的大部 分区域。 这些都是古老的原始森林，从冰川 时代就生长在这里。 特里波耶西部就都是这样的森林。 但是东部的气候要干燥得多。 如此干燥的气候不适宜森林，其水 分仅够维持草原的生长。

paragraphs.en: So a giant prairie ecosystem formed. In fact this grassland is called the "Eurasian Steppe" and it goes for thousands of miles. Here is a satellite image of Eurasia showing you where the steppe grasslands are located. As you can see the steppe goes all the way from the Hungarian plain in the West to the Mongolian grasslands in the East. To the north of the Steppe are the great Boreal forests of the Russian Taiga, and to the south are the great centralAsian Gobi and Taklamakan deserts. The steppe is an in-between sort of ecosystem.
paragraphs.zh: 于是东部孕育出了草原生态系统。 实际上这片草原被称为“欧亚大草 原”，绵延数千里。 这张欧亚大陆的卫星图显示了这 片大草原的位置。 你可以看到草原从西边的匈牙利 平原一直延伸到东边的蒙古大草 原。 草原北部是俄罗斯北方的针叶林， 南部是中亚的戈壁滩和塔克拉玛 干大沙漠。 这片草原生态系统居于中央。

paragraphs.en: Wetter than a true desert, there is enough rainfall for grass, but not enough for trees. Imagine you are living 7000 years ago. There are no cars or even horses that you can ride. The only way for people to travel is by walking. For these ancient humans, the steppe must have been a terrifying place. It makes you feel small when you are on it. Food and water are hard to find, and you have to walk for days and days to get anywhere different. For ancient people, these grasslands were basically a desert.
paragraphs.zh: 比沙漠湿润，足够生草，却又不足 以长出树木形成森林。 想象你生活在 7000 年前。 你没有车坐，甚至没有马骑。 人们唯一的交通方式就是步行。 对于那些古人来说，大草原一定是 一片恐怖地带。 当你置身其中，你会深感自己的渺 小。 食物和水源难以寻觅，要走出草原 也需要连日的跋涉。 对古人而言，这片草原基本上就是 一片荒漠。

paragraphs.en: Now all of this changed around 4800 BC. That's because this is approximately the time that domesticated cows, sheep and goats first arrived in this part of the world. And these animals completely transformed the economy in this part of the world. Humans cannot eat grass, so for ancient hunter gatherers, a grassland was a largely useless environment. But goats, sheep and cattle CAN eat grass, and suddenly they made it possible for humans to live in this part of the world. You can almost think of these animals as machines that transformed the grass into useful products like meat, milk, yoghurt,
paragraphs.zh: 但是在公元前 4800 年，一切都改 变了。 因为大约在此时，人类开始驯养 牛、绵羊，和山羊了。 这些动物彻底改变了这片地区的 经济。 人类不能吃草，所以对于古代的狩 猎采集者来说，草原基本上是无法 利用的环境。 但是山羊、绵羊和牛是食草的，突 然间人类就得以在草原上生存了。 你可以把这些动物看成是把青草 转化为其他有用产品的机器，比如 说肉类、鲜奶、酸奶、奶酪、血、 骨头、骨髓、羊毛，还有皮革。

paragraphs.en: cheese, blood, bone, marrow, wool, and leather. Around the same time (about 4800 BC) people who were now living in the steppe began to tame horses. Perhaps this happened because as people began to live on the grasslands, they came into more contact with the horses. Maybe it happened because people were inspired by tame cows and sheep and goats and decided to see if the same could be done to horses. Recent genetic studies have also shown us something interesting about horses. Apparently all modern horses descend from at least 77 different wild female horses. But they only descend from 1 wild
paragraphs.zh: 在同一时期（约公元前 4800 年）， 生活在大草原上的人类开始驯化 马。 也许正是因为这些人生活在草原 上，他们能更多地接触到马。 也可能他们因为驯化了牛羊而受 到了启发，想看看马能否同样被驯 化。 最近的一些基因研究揭示了一些 关于马的有趣信息。 很明显所有现代的马加起来有至 少 77 个雌性祖先。 但它们都是同一匹公马的后代。

paragraphs.en: male horse. Agentle male horse that could be domesticated easily was such a rare find that it may have happened only one time. At first people did not ride horses. The first horses were tamed as a source of meat, milk and leather, just like cows. And probably horses were an attractive candidate for domestication because they will dig up snow and break ice to get at the grass during the winter months. So horses do a much better job surviving winter than do cows, sheep or goats. But over the next 1000 years, two major innovations completely transformed human transportation.
paragraphs.zh: 或许要找一匹易于驯服的温顺的 公马实在是太难了，以至于当时的 人们只找到了一匹。 起初人类并不骑马。 最早被驯化的马匹是用来食肉、挤 奶、剥皮的，就像牛一样。 然而在冬季，马在草原上能够刨开 冰雪找到青草。可能是这种特质让 人们很想驯化马。 所以说马比牛羊更能够耐受寒冬。 但是在接下来的 1000 年里，两项 重大技术创新彻底改变了人类的 交通方式。

paragraphs.en: About 500 years after humans first domesticated horses for meat, (so around 4200 BC), we begin to see evidence that people were beginning to ride horses as well. And then around 3500 BC, the wheel was invented. So now people could build crude wagons, hitch them to oxen, and carry a large amount of supplies. This had a profound impact on the society of the people who lived on the steppe. The first big change is the number of animals that people could herd. Alone herdsman on foot can only manage about 200 animals. But a herdsman on horseback can manage a herd that is as big as 500 animals.
paragraphs.zh: 有证据表明，大约在人类驯马以作 为食物的 500 年后（即约公元前 4200 年），人类开始骑马了。 后来，在公元前 3500 年左右，车 轮也问世了。 于是自此人们可以制作简陋的车 轮，并拴在牛身上，用以运送大宗 货物补给。 这对于草原上的社会产生了深刻 的影响。 第一个重大变化就是人类可以放 牧的牲畜数量。 一个牧人徒步只能管理 200 头牲 畜。 但是一个牧人骑马可以管住 500 头牲畜。

paragraphs.en: So horseback riding allowed herds to get 2 and a half times bigger! Additionally, horses and wagons now made the steppe accessible to these early humans. People could survive on the grasslands by eating their animals. They could keep up with their herds by riding horses. And they could carry their small children and large amounts of supplies with wagons. And grasslands are the fastest type of terrain for horsemen to cross. So, in a way, Horse Riding and Wheel turned the steppe-lands from a desert into a highway. And so, around 3000 BC, a new culture began to develop north of
paragraphs.zh: 所以骑马使得放牧的牲畜数量涨 了 2.5 倍。 此外，马和车让大草原天堑变通 途。 人们在草原上可以靠宰杀牲畜为 食。 靠骑马追赶自己的牧群。 还可以用车辆运载自己的幼子和 大量补给。 而且草原对骑马的人而言，是最利 于快速通行的地形。 所以总而言之，骑马与车轮将大草 原从沙漠变成了高速公路。 在大约公元前 3000 年，黑海北部 发展出了一种新的文化。

paragraphs.en: the Black Sea. Archaeologists call these people the "Yamna." We think it was these people who first spoke the original proto-Indo European language that half of the worlds languages now descend from. So we can call these people the Yamna or the Proto Indo-Europeans. Now, throughout all of human history humans have gotten their food in one of three ways. 1) The first was hunting and gathering 2) The second was farming 3) And the third was herding of pastoralism. The Yamna people were the very
paragraphs.zh: 考古学家称他们为“亚穆纳人”。 我们认为这些人是最早开始讲原 始印欧语的人。如今世界上的语言 有一半都源于这种语言。 因此我们称这些人为亚穆纳人或 原始印欧人。 从古至今，在整个人类史上，人类 获取食物的方式无非三种。 1）狩猎采集 2）农耕 3）畜牧 亚穆纳人是世界上最早的靠畜牧

paragraphs.en: first in the world to learn how to survive in this third way. Around the same time, the Yamna people went through another big change. They developed the ability to drink milk as adults. Now before we talk about that change, I need to explain some science. You see, all humans can drink milk as babies, because we have to drink our mother's milk. But milk contains a special kind of sugar called "lactose." In order to digest lactose the body needs to produce a special enzyme called "lactase" Now, 10,000 years ago, when we
paragraphs.zh: 生存的人类。 大约在同一时期，亚穆纳人还经历 了另一项重大变化。 他们拥有了成年后喝奶的能力。 在我们讨论这项变化之前，我需要 阐释一些科学背景知识。 大家知道，人类在婴儿期都可以喝 奶，因为我们必须喝母乳。 但是奶中有一种特殊的糖类，叫做 “乳糖”。 要消化乳糖，人体需要一种叫做 “乳糖酶”的特殊酶。 在 10,000 年前，人类还过着狩猎

paragraphs.en: were all still hunter gatherers, nobody drank milk as an adult. Because obviously once you stop drinking your mother's milk, where are you going to get milk in the wild? So it was a waste of energy for the body to keep producing the lactase enzyme, and so the body stopped making the enzyme once you were a few years old. These people are called lactose intolerant. If you drink milk after your body stops producing the lactase enzyme, your digestive tract cannot break down the lactose sugar and you will have stomach problems. But about 5000 years ago, some people developed a mutation that
paragraphs.zh: 采集的生活，没有人能在成年后喝 奶。 原因显而易见，一旦你不再吃妈妈 的母乳，你还能到哪里弄到奶吃 呢？ 这样一来，人体再生产乳糖酶就是 浪费能量了，所以人在几岁大时， 体内就没有乳糖酶了。 这些人患有乳糖不耐症。 如果你在身体停止分泌乳糖酶后 喝牛奶，你的消化道不能分解乳 糖，就会有胃病。 但大约在 5000 年前，一些人发生 了突变，他们的身体在成年后会继

paragraphs.en: made their body continue to produce the lactase enzyme into adulthood. This meant that these people could drink milk for their whole lives. These people are called lactose tolerant. The gene which tells the body to stop producing lactase is found on the 2nd Chromosome. There have actually been two different mutations which make this gene stop working. One developed in Europe and the other developed inAfrica. The European one probably happened first.And the people who had this mutation kept producing lactase into adulthood and could drink milk. They were lactose tolerant.
paragraphs.zh: 续产生乳糖酶。 这意味着这些人一生都可以喝牛 奶。这些人具备乳糖耐受性的特 征。 科学家们在第二染色体上发现了 通知身体停止产生乳糖酶的基因。 实际上，有两种突变导致该基因停 止工作。一种发展自欧洲，一种在 非洲。 这种突变很可能率先发生在欧洲。 而那些具有这种突变的人会在成 年后继续分泌乳糖酶，可以喝牛 奶。他们具有乳糖耐受的特征。

paragraphs.en: But as this map shows you, not everyone in the world carries this mutation. Many people still have the original copy of the gene, which makes the body stop producing the enzyme. As you can see from this map. Europe is the part of the world with the highest concentration of this mutation, because milk has been an important part of the European diet for 5000 years. But in SoutheastAsia and China, the mutation is less common, because people did not traditionally drink milk in this part of the world. So East Asians are much more likely to be lactose intolerant than Northern Europeans. But remember there is nothing wrong with lactose intolerant
paragraphs.zh: 但正如这张地图所显示的，并不是 世界上每个人都携带这种突变。许 多人仍然拥有该基因的原始副本， 这使得身体停止产生乳糖酶。 你可以从这张地图上看到，欧洲是 世界上这种突变最为集中的地区， 因为牛奶在欧洲饮食中已经有 5000 年的历史了。 但在东南亚和中国，这种变异并不 常见，因为在这些地区，人们通常 不喝牛奶。 所以东亚人比北欧人更容易产生 乳糖不耐症。 但请记住，乳糖不耐症患者没有任 何问题，乳糖不耐是人类的原始状

paragraphs.en: people, lactose intolerance is the original human condition. Anyway, lactose tolerance has evolved at least 3 times in the last 5000 years. But we think it was here, among the Yamna people on the north coast of the black sea where it first evolved. Sometime around 3500 BC. This mutation was really important. Here's why. Atypical 600-kilogram cow will feed you only if you kill it. After butchering the cow you will get, on average, 250 kilograms of beef, 75 kilograms of fat and 24 kilograms of bone marrow. All of that food contains 1.5 million calories. That's enough to feed 2
paragraphs.zh: 态。 总之，在过去的 5000 年里，乳糖 耐受性至少进化了 3 次。 但我们认为，第一次进化发生在黑 海北海岸，约公元前 3500 年。 这个突变极其重要。 为什么会这样呢？ 一头 600 公斤重的牛，只有在你杀 死它之后，它才能喂饱你。 在宰杀牛后，通常会产生 250 公斤 牛肉、75 公斤脂肪和 24 公斤骨髓。 所有这些食物共含有 150 万卡路 里。这足以让 2 个人以每顿 2000

paragraphs.en: people on a 2000 calorie diet for 1 year. In other words, 1 cow has enough meat to feed 2 people for 1 year. But, what if you can DRINK the milk instead of killing it? Well a 600-kilogram dairy cow can produce about 12 kilograms of milk each day. That's about 4,300 kilograms of milk each year. 4300 kilograms of milk contains 2.6 million calories. That is enough energy to feed 3 people for a year. Milk drinking allows you to feed 50% more people with the same cow, and you don't even have to kill the cow. So horseback riding allows you to
paragraphs.zh: 卡路里的标准吃 1 年。 换句话说，一头牛的牺牲可以养活 2 个人 1 年。 但是，如果你能喝牛奶而不不需要 杀死牛呢？ 一头 600 公斤的奶牛每天可以产 出大约 12 公斤的牛奶，每年大约 可以产出 4300 公斤的牛奶。 4300 公斤的牛奶含有 260 万卡路 里。 这些能量足够养活 3 个人一年。 喝牛奶可以让你用同样的牛多喂 养 50%的人，而且还不用杀牛。 骑马可以让你拥有更大的牛群，而

paragraphs.en: have larger herds, and milk drinking allows you to feed more people with the same number of animals. These developments led to big changes. For comparison, a herder who has to be on foot can only manage about 200 cows. Since you cannot kill all your cows for food and have to keep some alive for breeding, those 200 cows can only feed about 200 people. But a lactose tolerant herder can keep all his cows alive for milking and he can manage a larger herd. The lactose tolerant horse-back herder can manage a herd of 500 cows, which can feed 2000 people. In other words, horseback riding and lactose tolerance allowed the Yamna population to get at least 10 times bigger.
paragraphs.zh: 喝牛奶可以让你用同样数量的动 物喂养更多的人。这些发展导致了 巨大的变化。 相比之下，一个步行的牧民只能管 理大约 200 头牛。既然你不能为了 食物而杀死你所有的牛，并且为了 繁殖，那 200 头牛只能养活大约 200 人。 但是一个乳糖耐受的牧民可以让 他所有的牛都活着挤奶，他可以管 理更大的牛群。一个会骑马的乳糖 耐受牧民可以管理 500 头牛，这些 奶牛可以养活 2000 人。 换句话说，骑马和乳糖耐受性使亚 穆纳的人口至少增加了 10 倍。

paragraphs.en: Therefore, between 3000 and 2000 BC, the population of the Yamna people exploded. They also became expert horsemen. And they had plenty of protein in their diet from drinking all that milk, and so their bodies were bigger than their neighbors. They also frequently fought each other for control of herds and land, so the Yamna also became skilled at warfare. And once the population became too large, they began pushing out of their ancestral homeland and moved into the rest of Eurasia. The combination of number, size, horses and fighting skill made the Yamna unstoppable, and everywhere they went, they tended
paragraphs.zh: 因此，在公元前 3000-2000 年之 间，亚穆纳人的数量激增。 亚穆纳人还成为了专业骑士。 由于他们喝牛奶，饮食中含有大量 的蛋白质，他们的身材比邻居更高 大。 他们也经常为了争夺畜群和土地 而互相争斗，所以亚穆纳也变得擅 长战争。 一旦人口过多，他们就开始离开祖 传的家园，来到了欧亚大陆的其他 地方。 数量、体型、马匹和战斗技巧的结 合使亚穆纳人所向披靡，无论他们 走到哪里，他们都能压倒当地的 人，成为主导群体。

paragraphs.en: to overwhelm the local population and become the dominant group. And of course they took their Indo-European language with them. Now over time the language spoken by these different Indo-European groups began to change. And the Yamna began to think of themselves as separate people. Eventually they evolved into several different groups who will be important to the rest of our history: The Celts, the Italics, the Germans, the Greeks, the Balkans, the Hittites, the Slavs, the Baltics, the Scythians, the Tocarians, the Medes, the Indo-Aryans and theArmenians. These are all different groups who descended from the original Yamna people over about 1000 years.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，他们的印欧语也跟随着人群 扩散了开来。 随着时间的推移，印欧语系中不同 的语言开始发生变化。 并且亚穆纳人开始认为自己是独 立的民族。 最终，他们进化成若干不同的群 体，对我们接下来的历史发展非常 重要： 凯尔特人，意大利人，德国人，希 腊人，巴尔干人，赫梯人，斯拉夫 人，波罗海人，斯基提亚人，托卡 人，米德斯，印度雅利安人和亚美 尼亚人。 这些不同的民族，经历了约 1000 年，从原始的亚穆纳人种独立开 来。

paragraphs.en: This image shows you how the original Indo-European language of the Yamna developed into many languages that are spoken today. For example, one branch of Proto Indo European developed into Italic. Italic developed into Latin, which was the language of the Romans. And then Latin developed into the modern languages: French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, Romanian, and Catalan. Another branch of Indo European developed into Proto-Germanic. One branch of that language developed into West Germanic. One branch of West Germanic developed intoAnglo-Frisian. That developed into Old English. Then under the
paragraphs.zh: 这张图片向你展示了亚穆纳的原 始印欧语是如何发展成今天所说 的多种语言的。 比如，原始印欧语的一个分支发展 成了意大利语族。 意大利语族又发展成了拉丁语，也 就是罗马人使用的语言。 然后拉丁语又发展出了多种现代 语言：法语，西班牙语，意大利语， 葡萄牙语，罗马尼亚语和加泰罗尼 亚语。 原始印欧语的另一个分支发展成 为了原始日耳曼语。 这种语言的一个分支发展为西日 尔曼语支。西日尔曼语支的一个分 支发展成为英弗语支，继而发展成 古英语。然后在法语的影响下，古 英语发展成中古英语，而中古英语

paragraphs.en: influence of French, Old English developed into Middle English, and Middle English developed into Modern English, the language I am speaking to you right now. In fact, this map shows us where Indo European languages are spoken in modern Eurasia. As you can see the language of Europe, India, Persia and Russia are all Indo-European. But because the Europeans all spoke Indo-European languages, and Europe colonized the world from the 1500s to the 1800s, Indo-European languages are now spoken inAmerica,Africa and Oceania. This map shows you which countries speak Indo-European languages. Dark blue means it is the
paragraphs.zh: 发展成为现代英语，也就是现在我 对你们说的语言。 事实上，这张地图向我们展示了在 现代欧亚大陆上，印欧语的使用范 围。 正如你所看到的，欧洲、印度、波 斯和俄罗斯的语言都属于印欧语 系。 但由于欧洲人都说印欧语，而欧洲 人从 16-19 世纪在全世界各地殖 民，印欧语系现在在美国、非洲和 大洋洲都有使用。 这张地图像你展示了哪些国家使 用印欧语系。印欧语在深蓝色区域 是主要语言；在浅蓝色区域是第二

paragraphs.en: main language, and light blue means it is a secondary language. As you can see, today nearly 50% of the world speaks an Indo-European language. In other words, they speak a language that comes from the language of those first herders on the north shore of the Black Sea, 5000 years ago. I hope you enjoyed that lesson on the Indo-Europeans. Now I have two recommendations for you if you'd like to learn more. The first is the History of English Podcast by Kevin Stroud. This podcast goes into extraordinary detail about the evolution and history of the English language, and in each episode you learn new things about where certain words came from.
paragraphs.zh: 语言。 正如你所见，今天世界上几乎有一 半的人说的是印欧语。换句话说， 他们说的语言来自于 5000 年前在 黑海北岸的第一批牧民的语言。 我希望你能喜欢印欧语系的课程。 如果想了解更多，我有两个推荐。 第一个是凯文·斯特劳德的《英语 历史》播客。该播客详细介绍了英 语的演变和历史，在每一集里，你 都能学到一些关于某些词的新内 容。

paragraphs.en: His first few episodes go into great detail about the Indo-Europeans, since of course their language was the distant ancestor of English. I would also like to recommend a great book about the Indo-Europeans called The Horse, The Wheel and Language by David Anthony. Okay, thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 他在前几集对印欧语系进行了详 细的介绍，因为它是英语的遥远祖 先。 我还想推荐一本关于印欧人的好 书：《马，轮子和语言》，作者是大 卫·安东尼。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第十二讲：当其他文明闪耀时，欧洲在干什么？

paragraphs.en: Hello, and welcome back to our lecture series on World History.
